$$e^{t/\tau}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}y = \dfrac{KA}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}$$

This is the exact derivative of $\left(e^{t/\tau}\,y\right)$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(e^{t/\tau}\,y\right) = \dfrac{KA}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$:

$$e^{t/\tau}\,y(t) - e^{0}\cdot y(0) = KA\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

Apply the initial condition $y(0) = 0$ (zero initial output for a recorder at rest):

$$e^{t/\tau}\,y(t) = KA\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

Multiply both sides by $e^{-t/\tau}$:

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\,}$$

**Verification of key points:**

- At $t = \tau$: $y(\tau) = KA(1 - e^{-1}) = KA(1 - 0.368) = 0.632\,KA$ — this is the **63.2 % rule**.
- At $t = 5\tau$: $y(5\tau) = KA(1 - e^{-5}) = 0.9933\,KA$ — recorder is effectively in steady state.
- Final value: $y(\infty) = KA$ — matches the static sensitivity $K$ times the step amplitude $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> The number $5\tau$ is universally adopted as the *practical settling time* for a first-order system because $e^{-5} \approx 0.0067$ (i.e., 0.67 % error).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Frequency Response

Replace the differential operator with $j\omega$ (the phasor substitution):

$$\tau(j\omega)Y + Y = K X \quad\Longrightarrow\quad Y(1 + j\omega\tau) = KX$$

$$\dfrac{Y(j\omega)}{X(j\omega)} = \dfrac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$

The **magnitude ratio** is:

$$\vert G(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}}$$

The **phase angle** is:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$$

**Key deductions:**

- At low frequency ($\omega \to 0$): $\vert G \vert = K$, $\phi = 0^\circ$ — recorder behaves as a pure gain $K$.
- At high frequency ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert G \vert \to 0$, $\phi \to -90^\circ$ — recorder attenuates the signal.
- At the **break frequency** $\omega_b = 1/\tau$ (i.e., $f_b = 1/(2\pi\tau)$): $\vert G \vert = K/\sqrt{2} = 0.707K$ — this defines the **−3 dB bandwidth**.

The 3-dB point is derived from the definition:

$$20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{K/\sqrt{2}}{K}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -3.01\ \text{dB} \approx -3\ \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering consequence in bio-mechatronics:** the high-frequency cutoff of an ECG amplifier is set by choosing $\tau$ such that $f_b = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ falls just above 150 Hz, ensuring QRS sharpness is preserved.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Second-Order System Step Response and Overshoot

A second-order recording system (e.g., a galvanometric pen recorder, a force-balance transducer) follows:

$$\dfrac{d^2y}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \omega_n^2 y = \omega_n^2 K\,x(t)$$

For a step input $x(t) = A$ and underdamped condition $\zeta < 1$, the solution is:

$$y(t) = KA\left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin\!\left(\omega_d t + \theta\right)\right]$$

where:
- $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.
- $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$ is the **phase shift**.

**Derivation of peak overshoot:** The peak occurs when $\dot{y}(t) = 0$ for the first time after $t = 0$. The first peak is at:

$$t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d}$$

Substitute $t_p$ into the response equation. The exponential envelope is $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_p}$ and the sine term evaluates to $-1$ (since the argument is $\pi + \theta$ whose sine is $-\sin\theta$):

$$M_p = y(t_p) - y(\infty) = KA\left[\dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_p}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\cdot\sin\theta\right] - 0$$

Using $\sin\theta = \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$M_p = KA\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_p} = KA\,e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Normalizing by the steady-state value $KA$:

$$\boxed{\,\%\text{Overshoot} = 100\cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\,}$$

**Numerical check** for $\zeta = 0.4$:

$\%M_p = 100\cdot e^{-0.4\pi/\sqrt{1-0.16}} = 100\cdot e^{-0.4\pi/0.9165} = 100\cdot e^{-1.371} = 100\cdot 0.254 = 25.4\,\%$

This matches the classical control-systems table value for $\zeta = 0.4$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Settling Time (2 % Criterion)

Settling time is defined as the time after which the response remains within $\pm 2\,\%$ of the final value. The envelope of the underdamped response is:

$$\text{Envelope}(t) = KA\left(1 \pm \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)$$

The condition $\vert e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \vert \le 0.02$ gives, for moderate damping ($\zeta \le 0.7$ where $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$):

$$e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_s} \le 0.02$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\zeta\omega_n t_s \le \ln(0.02) = -3.912$$

$$t_s \ge \dfrac{3.912}{\zeta\omega_n} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}\,}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Recording System Specifications for Common Bio-Signals

| Bio-Signal | Amplitude | Frequency Range | Required Sensitivity | Required CMRR | Typical Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | 0.5 – 5 mV | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 1 V/mV | $\geq$ 80 dB | Ag/AgCl surface electrodes |
| EEG | 10 – 100 µV | 0.5 – 100 Hz | 10⁴ V/V (100 dB) | $\geq$ 100 dB | Scalp electrodes |
| EMG | 50 µV – 5 mV | 20 – 5000 Hz | 1000 V/V | $\geq$ 60 dB | Needle / surface electrodes |
| Blood Pressure (arterial) | 40 – 300 mmHg | 0 – 50 Hz | 5 µV/V/mmHg | $\geq$ 80 dB | Strain-gauge bridge |
| SpO₂ | 70 – 100 % | 0 – 5 Hz (pulse) | 1 %/% | N/A | Photoplethysmograph (PPG) |
| Body Temperature | 32 – 42 °C | 0 – 0.1 Hz | 0.1 V/°C | N/A | Thermistor / thermocouple |
| Respiratory Airflow | 0 – 600 L/min | 0 – 20 Hz | 1 mV/(L/min) | N/A | Pneumotachograph |
| Heart Sound (PCG) | $\sim$ 1 Pa | 20 – 2000 Hz | 10⁴ V/Pa | N/A | Microphone / stethoscope |

> [!NOTE]
> The frequency range of the bio-signal directly determines the **required bandwidth** of the recording system. Choosing a recording channel with insufficient bandwidth clips the high-frequency information (e.g., QRS notching in ECG, high-frequency harmonics in PCG murmurs).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Biomedical Recording System

The figure below maps the canonical signal flow from the patient's body to the display, highlighting the points at which static and dynamic characteristics are imposed.

<!-- Mermaid safeguard applied: all node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed; no reserved keywords; all labels are clean uppercase text. -->

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[PATIENT] --> A1[BIOSENSOR / ELECTRODE]
    A1 --> A2[PRE-AMPLIFIER]
    A2 --> A3[ISOLATION STAGE]
    A3 --> A4[MAIN AMPLIFIER]
    A4 --> A5[FILTER BANK]
    A5 --> A6[ADC SAMPLING STAGE]
    A6 --> A7[RECORDING MEDIA / DISPLAY]

    B1[SENSITIVITY GAIN K] -.controls.-> A2
    B1 -.controls.-> A4
    B2[CMRR DESIGN] -.controls.-> A2
    B3[BANDWIDTH FILTER fc] -.controls.-> A5
    B4[SAMPLING RATE fs] -.controls.-> A6
    B5[DRIFT COMPENSATION] -.controls.-> A4

    classDef block fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1a3d7c,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0b1e3f;
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#a86b00,stroke-width:1.2px,color:#5a3a00;
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 block;
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 ctrl;
```

## 4.2 Static Characteristics — Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    SC[STATIC CHARACTERISTICS] --> SC1[ACCURACY]
    SC --> SC2[PRECISION]
    SC --> SC3[SENSITIVITY]
    SC --> SC4[LINEARITY]
    SC --> SC5[RESOLUTION]
    SC --> SC6[HYSTERESIS]
    SC --> SC7[DRIFT]

    SC1 --> SC1A[ERROR vs TRUE VALUE]
    SC2 --> SC2A[REPRODUCIBILITY]
    SC3 --> SC3A[SLOPE OF CALIBRATION CURVE]
    SC4 --> SC4A[DEVIATION FROM BEST FIT LINE]
    SC5 --> SC5A[SMALLEST DETECTABLE STEP]
    SC6 --> SC6A[UP DOWN PATH DIFFERENCE]
    SC7 --> SC7A[ZERO DRIFT SCALE DRIFT]

    classDef main fill:#e0f7e0,stroke:#1d6f1d,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0a3a0a;
    classDef sub fill:#f4f9ff,stroke:#345a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#102a4d;
    class SC,SC1,SC2,SC3,SC4,SC5,SC6,SC7 main;
    class SC1A,SC2A,SC3A,SC4A,SC5A,SC6A,SC7A sub;
```

## 4.3 Dynamic Characteristics — Step and Frequency Response Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DYNAMIC CHARACTERISTICS] --> DC1[STEP RESPONSE]
    DC --> DC2[FREQUENCY RESPONSE]

    DC1 --> DC1A[TIME CONSTANT TAU]
    DC1 --> DC1B[RISE TIME TR]
    DC1 --> DC1C[SETTLING TIME TS]
    DC1 --> DC1D[OVERSHOOT MP]
    DC1 --> DC1E[DAMPING RATIO ZETA]

    DC2 --> DC2A[BANDWIDTH F3DB]
    DC2 --> DC2B[NATURAL FREQUENCY WN]
    DC2 --> DC2C[PHASE SHIFT PHI]
    DC2 --> DC2D[GAIN MARGIN GM]

    classDef root fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#7a1d1d,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#3a0a0a;
    classDef branch fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#a86b00,stroke-width:1.2px,color:#5a3a00;
    class DC root;
    class DC1,DC2 branch;
    classDef leaf fill:#eef9ff,stroke:#345a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#0c1d3a;
    class DC1A,DC1B,DC1C,DC1D,DC1E,DC2A,DC2B,DC2C,DC2D leaf;
```

## 4.4 Indicative Step-Response Curves for a Second-Order System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SR[STEP INPUT OF AMPLITUDE A APPLIED AT T=0] --> SYS[SECOND ORDER RECORDING SYSTEM]
    SYS --> R1[OVERDAMPED ZETA GT 1 SLOW RISE NO OVERSHOOT]
    SYS --> R2[CRITICALLY DAMPED ZETA EQUALS 1 FASTEST NO OVERSHOOT]
    SYS --> R3[UNDERDAMPED ZETA LT 1 OSCILLATION WITH OVERSHOOT]
    SYS --> R4[UNDAMPED ZETA EQUALS 0 PURE OSCILLATION NEVER SETTLES]

    classDef src fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#444,stroke-width:1.2px,color:#222;
    classDef sys fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#a85a00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#4d2a00;
    classDef res fill:#d6ecff,stroke:#1c4a82,stroke-width:1.2px,color:#0a2240;
    class SR src;
    class SYS sys;
    class R1,R2,R3,R4 res;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the figure matters:** in bio-mechatronics, a *critically damped* pen recorder is preferred for ECG strip charts so that successive QRS complexes are clearly separated without ringing. However, a slightly *underdamped* setting is sometimes used in phonocardiography (heart-sound recording) to enhance the sharpness of murmur onsets.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define the following with one biomedical example each: (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Hysteresis, (iii) Drift.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Sensitivity:** It is defined as the ratio of change in output to the corresponding change in input of the recording system. Mathematically, $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$. *Biomedical example:* An ECG pre-amplifier with sensitivity of 1 V/mV means a 1 mV cardiac signal produces a 1 V output.

**(ii) Hysteresis:** It is the maximum difference in output observed for the same value of input, when the input is approached from opposite directions (increasing vs decreasing). It is caused by friction, backlash, or magnetic hysteresis in the transducer. *Biomedical example:* A mechanical spirometer pen recorder tracing a forced vital capacity manoeuvre may show a small loop between inspiration and expiration curves due to pen-friction hysteresis.

**(iii) Drift:** It is the undesired change in output of the recording system over a period of time when the input is held constant (zero drift), or the change in the calibration slope (sensitivity drift). *Biomedical example:* A strain-gauge blood-pressure transducer shows zero drift of a few mmHg/hour due to temperature changes and bridge imbalance, requiring periodic re-zeroing.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between static and dynamic characteristics of a recording system. List any four dynamic characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Characteristics | Dynamic Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| Input condition | Constant or slowly varying | Time-varying, transient |
| What is measured | Accuracy, linearity, sensitivity | Speed, fidelity, bandwidth |
| Test signal | Calibrated DC levels | Step, ramp, sinusoidal |
| Typical use | Calibration, quality check | Real-time physiological recording |

**Four dynamic characteristics:**

1. **Time constant ($\tau$)** — time for the step response to reach 63.2 % of final value.
2. **Rise time ($t_r$)** — time for the output to rise from 10 % to 90 % of its final value.
3. **Settling time ($t_s$)** — time to settle within a tolerance band (e.g., $\pm 2\,\%$) of the final value.
4. **Bandwidth / Frequency response** — range of frequencies over which the amplitude response stays within $\pm 3$ dB of the mid-band gain.

*(Acceptable alternatives: overshoot, damping ratio, natural frequency, phase shift, dynamic error.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]
**(a)** Define *sensitivity*, *linearity*, *hysteresis*, and *drift* as static characteristics of a recording system. Explain how each is measured, and discuss their relevance to a clinical ECG recorder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order recording channel has a static sensitivity $K = 2$ V/unit and a time constant $\tau = 0.1$ s. A step input of amplitude $A = 5$ units is applied at $t = 0$.
   (i) Derive the expression for the output $y(t)$.
   (ii) Find the value of $y(t)$ at $t = 0.1$ s and at $t = 0.5$ s.
   (iii) Compute the −3 dB bandwidth of the channel.
   (iv) State the rise time (10 % – 90 %) for this first-order system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Sensitivity (1 mark):** Sensitivity $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ is the slope of the calibration curve. For a clinical ECG recorder, a sensitivity of **1 V/mV** is standard so that a 1 mV QRS produces a 1 V pen deflection on a strip-chart (1 cm per mV on a standard ECG grid). It is measured by feeding a known calibration pulse (e.g., 1 mV, 1 Hz square wave) and verifying the output deflection.

**Linearity (2 marks):** Linearity is the deviation of the actual output-input relationship from a best-fit straight line. It is quantified as

$$\%\text{NL} = \dfrac{(\Delta y)_{\max\text{ from ideal}}}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100$$

For an ECG recorder, the linearity should be **within $\pm 2\,\%$** so that the R-wave amplitude of a 2.5 mV signal is not interpreted as 3.0 mV. Linearity is checked by applying a staircase of known amplitudes (e.g., 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 mV) and plotting output vs input.

**Hysteresis (2 marks):** Hysteresis is the maximum difference in output for the *same* input approached from increasing vs decreasing directions:

$$\%\text{Hys} = \dfrac{(y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow})_{\max}}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100$$

In an ECG pen recorder, hysteresis causes the upstroke and downstroke of a QRS spike to take slightly different paths, producing a small open loop. Standard ECG recorders specify hysteresis $\le 1\,\%$ of FSO. It is minimised by using servo-driven (thermal-array) printers instead of mechanical pen-galvanometer systems.

**Drift (2 marks):** Drift is the slow change in output with no change in input, due to component ageing, temperature, or power-supply variation.

- **Zero drift** is the change in baseline (e.g., baseline wander in ECG at 0.1 Hz). It is countered by **AC coupling** with a high-pass filter at **0.05 Hz**.
- **Sensitivity drift** is the change in calibration gain. It is countered by periodic **1 mV calibration pulses** built into the recorder front panel.

**Clinical relevance (built into all four sub-points):** an ECG recorder with poor sensitivity hides low-amplitude P-waves; poor linearity mis-reads ST elevation; high hysteresis blurs the QRS onset (used to time intervals); drift causes baseline wander that masks the true ST segment — all of which can lead to misdiagnosis of myocardial infarction or arrhythmia.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $K = 2$ V/unit, $\tau = 0.1$ s, $A = 5$ units.

**(i) Derivation of $y(t)$ — 2 marks**

The standard first-order step response derived in Section 3.1 is:

$$y(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$y(t) = (2)(5)\left(1 - e^{-t/0.1}\right) = 10\left(1 - e^{-10t}\right)\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Evaluation at specific instants — 2 marks**

At $t = 0.1$ s:

$$y(0.1) = 10\left(1 - e^{-10 \times 0.1}\right) = 10(1 - e^{-1}) = 10(1 - 0.3679) = 10 \times 0.6321 = 6.321\ \text{V}$$

*(This is the 63.2 % point — confirms the definition of time constant.)*

At $t = 0.5$ s:

$$y(0.5) = 10\left(1 - e^{-10 \times 0.5}\right) = 10(1 - e^{-5}) = 10(1 - 0.00674) = 10 \times 0.9933 = 9.933\ \text{V}$$

*(This is the 5τ point — practically at steady state.)*

**(iii) Bandwidth — 2 marks**

The −3 dB frequency for a first-order system is:

$$f_{b} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi(0.1)} = \dfrac{1}{0.6283} = 1.5915\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.59\ \text{Hz}$$

**(iv) Rise time — 1 mark**

For a first-order system, the 10 %–90 % rise time is:

$$t_r = 2.2\,\tau = 2.2 \times 0.1 = 0.22\ \text{s}$$

*(This is the universal control-systems relation $t_r = 2.2\tau$ for a first-order low-pass system.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students confuse the *static sensitivity* $K$ with the *dynamic gain*. Remember that $K$ is the **asymptotic final value** of the output per unit input at steady state — it is **not** the initial slope. Also, when asked for the −3 dB bandwidth, write the *numerical value with units*; do not leave the answer in symbolic form.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, Alternative Choice]
**(a)** What is meant by *dynamic characteristics* of a recording system? Derive the step response of a first-order system and explain the physical significance of the time constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second-order recording galvanometer has a natural frequency $f_n = 50$ Hz and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.3$.
   (i) Calculate the percentage overshoot of the step response.
   (ii) Find the rise time (0 % to 100 %) of the response.
   (iii) Estimate the settling time within $\pm 2\,\%$.
   (iv) Comment on the suitability of this galvanometer for recording a 1 kHz triangular waveform. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Dynamic characteristics describe the *time-domain* and *frequency-domain* behaviour of the recording system when the input is rapidly changing. They quantify how fast the output follows the input and how faithfully it is reproduced.

**Derivation of the step response (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, for a first-order ODE $\tau \dot y + y = Kx(t)$ with $x(t) = A\,u(t)$ and $y(0) = 0$:

$$y(t) = KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

(Show the integrating-factor method step by step as in Section 3.1.)

**Physical significance of the time constant (2 marks):** The time constant $\tau$ is the time required for the step response to reach **63.2 %** of its final value. It is a direct measure of the **speed of response**: a *small* $\tau$ implies a *fast* recording system. For a biomedical recorder (e.g., a thermistor thermometer measuring skin temperature), $\tau$ determines how quickly the system can follow a changing temperature. It is the time needed for the output to fall to **36.8 %** of the *initial error* $(KA - y(t))$ — equivalently, every $\tau$ seconds the remaining error shrinks by a factor of $1/e \approx 0.368$.

**Domain-specific example:** for an ECG amplifier with $\tau = 0.01$ s, the −3 dB cutoff is $f_{-3\text{dB}} = 1/(2\pi\tau) \approx 15.9$ Hz — clearly inadequate for diagnostic ECG; the designer must reduce $\tau$ further to push the cutoff above 150 Hz.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_n = 50$ Hz, $\zeta = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Convert natural frequency to rad/s:**

$$\omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 314.16 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.09} = 314.16 \times \sqrt{0.91} = 314.16 \times 0.9539 = 299.69\ \text{rad/s}$$

**(i) Percentage overshoot — 2 marks**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.3\pi/\sqrt{0.91}}$$

$$= 100 \cdot e^{-0.3 \times 3.1416 / 0.9539} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.988} = 100 \times 0.3724 \approx 37.2\,\%$$

**(ii) Rise time — 2 marks**

Using the formula from Section 2.3 (line 13):

$$t_r = \dfrac{\pi - \theta}{\omega_d}\quad\text{where}\quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.9539}{0.3}\right) = \tan^{-1}(3.1797) = 1.266\ \text{rad}$$

$$t_r = \dfrac{\pi - 1.266}{299.69} = \dfrac{3.1416 - 1.266}{299.69} = \dfrac{1.876}{299.69} = 6.26 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} \approx 6.26\ \text{ms}$$

**(iii) Settling time — 1 mark**

$$t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n} = \dfrac{4}{0.3 \times 314.16} = \dfrac{4}{94.25} = 0.0424\ \text{s} \approx 42.4\ \text{ms}$$

**(iv) Suitability for 1 kHz triangular wave — 2 marks**

The fundamental frequency of a 1 kHz triangular wave is 1000 Hz. The galvanometer's −3 dB bandwidth is approximately $f_{-3\text{dB}} \approx f_n = 50$ Hz (for moderate damping). The triangular wave's harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th, … at 3 kHz, 5 kHz, 7 kHz, …) are all **far above** the bandwidth and will be **heavily attenuated**. The result will be a heavily rounded, sinusoidal-looking trace — the triangular shape will be lost. **Conclusion: the galvanometer is unsuitable** for recording 1 kHz triangular signals. A recorder with a bandwidth of at least 5–10 times the highest harmonic of interest (i.e., $\geq 50$ kHz) is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. Students frequently forget to convert $f_n$ in Hz to $\omega_n$ in rad/s before using the second-order formulas — every $2\pi$ error costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. Do not use the first-order $t_r = 2.2\tau$ for a second-order system; that formula applies only to single-pole systems.
> 3. When asked to *comment on suitability*, do not just say "no" — state the bandwidth limit numerically and link it to the signal's spectral content.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Mixing up static and dynamic characteristics in Part A** — if the question explicitly says "static", do not list rise time, overshoot, or settling time.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the formula sheet — KTU examiners explicitly deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Sign convention in overshoot** — overshoot is always a *positive* percentage, even though the response momentarily exceeds the setpoint. Do not write "−37 %" for a 37 % overshoot.
> 4. **Phase angle sign** — for a minimum-phase recorder, phase is always **negative** (output lags input). Writing $+90^\circ$ will be marked wrong.
> 5. **CMRR and SNR in dB** — both are *logarithmic* ratios. Do not compute the ratio and forget the $20\log_{10}$ conversion.
> 6. **Drift vs noise** — drift is *slow, deterministic* (predictable from temperature); noise is *fast, random* (Gaussian). Conflating them is a common conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of the Recording System**

- **Static Characteristics:** Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Linearity, Resolution, Threshold, Dead Zone, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Reproducibility, Drift (zero & scale-factor).
- **Dynamic Characteristics:** Time constant, Rise time, Settling time, Overshoot, Damping ratio, Natural frequency, Damped frequency, Bandwidth, Phase shift, Dynamic error, Lag, Fidelity.
- **First-order step response:** $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; 63.2 % rule; $5\tau$ settling rule.
- **First-order bandwidth:** $f_{-3\text{dB}} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$; rise time $t_r = 2.2\tau$.
- **Second-order step response:** $y(t) = KA\left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin(\omega_d t + \theta)\right]$.
- **% Overshoot:** $\%M_p = 100\,e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$; depends **only on $\zeta$**.
- **Settling time (2 %):** $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
- **CMRR (dB):** $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$; **SNR (dB):** $20\log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$.
- **Biomedical benchmarks:** ECG $\geq$ 80 dB CMRR, $\geq$ 5 MΩ input impedance, 0.05–150 Hz bandwidth; EEG $\geq$ 100 dB CMRR, 0.5–100 Hz.
- **Patient safety metrics:** leakage current $\leq$ 10 µA (Type CF cardiac).
- **Practical settling rule:** First-order $\to 5\tau$; Second-order underdamped $\to 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
- **Golden rule of bio-recording:** *Sensitivity $\times$ Bandwidth $\times$ CMRR* must be balanced against *patient safety, noise, and artefact rejection*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrocardiogram and Einthoven Triangle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT861 - BIO MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electrocardiogram and Einthoven Triangle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG) — Formal Definition The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive bioelectric recording that graphically represents the time-varying electrical potential differences generated by the depolarization and repolarization of the cardiac muscle (myocardium) over one cardiac cycle. It is acquired by placing surface electrodes on the limbs and/or chest and measuring the potential differences between specific pairs of electrodes, known as **leads**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):** > An ECG is the transthoracic interpretation of the electrical activity of the heart over a period of time, as detected by electrodes attached to the outer surface of the skin. Each electrode pair defines a *lead*, and 12 standard leads are conventionally used clinically. | Parameter | Standard Clinical Range | KTU Board Expectation | |---|---|---| | ECG signal amplitude | **0.5 mV to 5 mV** (peak) | Always quote in **mV** | | Frequency content | **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** | Mention dominant energy 0.5–40 Hz | | Standard paper speed | **25 mm/s** | 1 small box = 0.04 s, 1 big box = 0.20 s | | Calibration pulse | **10 mm = 1 mV** | Standardize before recording | ## 1.2 Einthoven's Triangle — Formal Definition **Einthoven's Triangle** is an imaginary inverted equilateral triangle drawn on the frontal plane of the body, with its vertices located at the three limb electrodes: **Right Arm (RA)**, **Left Arm (LA)**, and **Left Leg (LL)**. The center of this triangle represents the heart's electrical axis. The sides of the triangle represent the three **bipolar limb leads**: Lead I, Lead II, and Lead III. The triangle was proposed by **Willem Einthoven** (Nobel Prize, 1924), who formulated the foundational equations of ECG vector theory. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Heart as a Battery Inside a Bowl":** > Imagine your heart is a **battery (a dipole)** floating in a salt-water bowl (the conductive body tissue). The battery's positive and negative terminals keep switching polarity with every heartbeat. Now, place three electrodes on the rim of the bowl at the arm and leg positions — they form a triangle around the battery. > Each *side* of that triangle acts like a voltmeter measuring the *projection* of the battery's voltage along that direction. That's exactly what an ECG lead is — a directional voltmeter looking at the heart's electrical vector from a different angle. --- > [!TIP] > **Why Three Sides of a Triangle?** > Three non-collinear leads are mathematically the **minimum required** to uniquely determine any 2-D planar vector (the heart's frontal plane axis) — analogous to how two sides uniquely determine a point in a plane. Einthoven chose exactly this geometry for elegance and completeness. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Genesis of the ECG Signal — Why a Wave Appears The cardiac cycle produces the ECG through these sequential biophysical steps: 1. **SA Node Firing** — Sino-Atrial node initiates an action potential (~ −70 mV → +30 mV). 2. **Atrial Depolarization** — Wave propagates across atria → produces the **P wave**. 3. **AV Node Delay** — ~0.1 s delay allows ventricular filling. 4. **Ventricular Depolarization** — Bundle of His → Purkinje fibers → ventricular mass depolarizes → produces the **QRS complex** (largest deflection). 5. **Ventricular Repolarization** — Produces the **T wave** (asymmetric, broad). 6. **Atrial Repolarization** — Hidden inside QRS (Ta wave, usually invisible). > [!NOTE] > **Waveform Mnemonic:** *"**P**lease **Q**uite **R**un **S**lowly, my **T**ired **U**nderwear*" ## 2.2 The Standard 12-Lead ECG System The 12 leads comprise **3 bipolar limb leads** (Einthoven) + **3 augmented unipolar limb leads** (Goldberger) + **6 precordial chest leads** (Wilson). This module focuses on the first six. ## 2.3 Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Original Three) | Lead | Electrode (+ve) | Electrode (−ve) | Equation | Physiological View | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Lead I** | LA | RA | $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ | Lateral wall of LV | | **Lead II** | LL | RA | $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ | Inferior wall + LV | | **Lead III** | LL | LA | $V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ | Inferior wall + RV | ## 2.4 Einthoven's Law (The Foundation Equation) The single most important equation of this module: $$V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$$ Or in terms of electrode potentials: $$V_{LL} - V_{RA} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$ This is algebraically identically true — making it a **consistency check** for any ECG machine. ## 2.5 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger) These use a **modified central terminal** (average of two limb electrodes) as the negative reference: $$aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$ $$aVL = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$ $$aVF = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}$$ ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ | Lead I potential | Direct definition | | 2 | $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ | Lead II potential | Direct definition | | 3 | $V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ | Lead III potential | Direct definition | | 4 | $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ | **Einthoven's Law** | Consistency check | | 5 | $V_{LA} + V_{LL} + V_{RA} \approx 0$ | Wilson Central Terminal (reference) | Aug. leads | | 6 | $aVR + aVL + aVF = 0$ | Augmented lead identity | Diagnostic | | 7 | $aVR = -\frac{(V_I + V_{II})}{2}$ | Aug. VR in terms of bipolar | Substitution | | 8 | $\vec{H} = H_x \hat{i} + H_y \hat{j}$ | Cardiac vector in frontal plane | Axis determination | | 9 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H_y}{H_x}\right)$ | Mean QRS axis angle | Clinical | | 10 | $H_x = \dfrac{2 V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}{3}$ | X-component of heart vector | Vectorcardiography | | 11 | $H_y = \dfrac{V_{II} - V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Y-component of heart vector | Vectorcardiography | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering - **Cardiac Diagnostic Software** — Automated ECG analyzers rely on these equations for signal validation. - **Pacemaker Design** — Lead configurations determine sensing vectors. - **Defibrillator Synchronization** — R-wave detection across multiple leads. - **Wearable ECG Patches** (Apple Watch, AliveCor) — Use reduced lead sets derived from Einthoven's geometry. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Einthoven's Law **Given:** The definitions of the three bipolar leads: $$V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$ $$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$ $$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$ **To Prove:** $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ **Step 1:** Start from the right-hand side and expand each term. $$\begin{aligned} V_I + V_{III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\ &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\ &= -V_{RA} + V_{LL} \\ &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\ &= V_{II} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Einthoven's Law is an *algebraic identity*, not a physiological law. It is used as an **instrumentation consistency check**. If the three leads violate it, the machine has a calibration error. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Augmented Lead $aVR$ **Augmented lead vector concept:** $aVR$ looks at the heart *from the right arm*, with reference being the average of the other two limb electrodes. **Reference (Wilson's central terminal, modified):** $$V_{ref}^{aVR} = \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$ **Lead potential definition:** $$aVR = V_{RA} - V_{ref}^{aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$ **Step 1:** Express all terms using bipolar lead voltages. Using $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA} \Rightarrow V_{LA} = V_I + V_{RA}$ Using $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} \Rightarrow V_{LL} = V_{II} + V_{RA}$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the $aVR$ expression. $$\begin{aligned} aVR &= V_{RA} - \frac{(V_I + V_{RA}) + (V_{II} + V_{RA})}{2} \\ &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_I + V_{II} + 2 V_{RA}}{2} \\ &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2} - V_{RA} \\ &= -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Simplified Form:** $$\boxed{aVR = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Cardiac Vector Components Assume the heart acts as an equivalent dipole $\vec{H}$ located at the centroid of Einthoven's triangle. Place the origin at the heart. The leads measure projections of $\vec{H}$ along their respective axes. **Coordinate Setup:** - X-axis: Horizontal (towards Left Arm, LA) - Y-axis: Vertical (towards Left Leg, LL, downward) **Lead I vector:** Along $+X$ axis (LA − RA). $$V_I = k \cdot H_x \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_x \propto V_I$$ **Lead III vector:** From LA to LL, which makes a $60^\circ$ angle with the $+X$ axis. $$V_{III} = k \cdot (H_x \cos 60^\circ + H_y \sin 60^\circ)$$ **Lead II vector:** From RA to LL, which makes a $60^\circ$ angle with $+X$ axis too. $$V_{II} = k \cdot (H_x \cos 60^\circ - H_y \sin 60^\circ)$$ **Step 1:** Solve for $H_y$ by subtracting the two equations. $$\begin{aligned} V_{III} - V_{II} &= k \cdot \left[ (H_x \cos 60^\circ + H_y \sin 60^\circ) - (H_x \cos 60^\circ - H_y \sin 60^\circ) \right] \\ &= k \cdot (2 H_y \sin 60^\circ) \\ &= k \cdot H_y \sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \quad H_y &= \frac{V_{III} - V_{II}}{k \sqrt{3}} = -\frac{V_{II} - V_{III}}{k \sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$$ Using the convention $V_{II} - V_{III}$ gives the **positive Y-component** (downward positive). $$H_y = \frac{V_{II} - V_{III}}{k \sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $H_x$ by adding the two equations. $$\begin{aligned} V_{III} + V_{II} &= k \cdot (2 H_x \cos 60^\circ) \\ &= k \cdot H_x \\ \therefore \quad H_x &= \frac{V_{II} + V_{III}}{k} \end{aligned}$$ Using Einthoven's law $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$: $$H_x = \frac{V_I + 2 V_{III}}{k} = \frac{2 V_I + V_I + 2 V_{III}}{3k} = \frac{2 V_I + V_{II}}{3k}$$ $$\boxed{H_x = \frac{2 V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}{3k}, \quad H_y = \frac{V_{II} - V_{III}}{\sqrt{3}\, k}}$$ Where $k$ is a constant incorporating electrode geometry. **Mean Electrical Axis Angle:** $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{H_y}{H_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(V_{II} - V_{III})}{2 V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}\right)$$ --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** Given $V_I = 1.2$ mV, $V_{II} = 1.0$ mV, $V_{III} = -0.2$ mV. Verify Einthoven's law and find the augmented lead values. **Solution:** **Step 1: Verify Einthoven's Law** $$V_I + V_{III} = 1.2 + (-0.2) = 1.0 \text{ mV} = V_{II} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 2: Compute $aVR$** $$aVR = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2} = -\frac{1.2 + 1.0}{2} = -1.1 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3: Compute $aVL$** Using the analogous formula (derived by symmetry): $$aVL = V_I - \frac{V_{II}}{2} = 1.2 - 0.5 = 0.7 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4: Compute $aVF$** $$aVF = V_{II} - \frac{V_I}{2} = 1.0 - 0.6 = 0.4 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 5: Verify Augmented Identity** $$aVR + aVL + aVF = -1.1 + 0.7 + 0.4 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 6: Mean Electrical Axis** $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(V_{II} - V_{III})}{2 V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(1.2)}{3.2}\right) \approx 33.2^\circ$$ (Normal range: −30° to +90°) # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Einthoven Triangle — Physical Schematic ```mermaid graph TD RA[RA Right Arm]:::nodeA LA[LA Left Arm]:::nodeB LL[LL Left Leg]:::nodeC H[Cardiac Dipole at Centroid]:::nodeD RA ---|"Lead I (+ve at LA)"| LA LA ---|"Lead III (+ve at LL)"| LL RA ---|"Lead II (+ve at LL)"| LL H -.-> RA H -.-> LA H -.-> LL classDef nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef nodeB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef nodeC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef nodeD fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` **Visual interpretation:** RA at top-right, LA at top-left, LL at bottom-center forming an inverted equilateral triangle. The cardiac dipole sits at the centroid. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ECG Machine ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Electrodes
RA LA LL]:::stepA B[Lead Selector
Network]:::stepB C[Instrumentation
Amplifier
Gain ~1000]:::stepC D[Bandpass Filter
0.05 - 150 Hz]:::stepD E[ADC
Sampling 500 Hz]:::stepE F[Digital Signal
Processor]:::stepF G[Display /
Printer]:::stepG A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G classDef stepA fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333 classDef stepB fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#333 classDef stepC fill:#C1FFD7,stroke:#333 classDef stepD fill:#FFFFC1,stroke:#333 classDef stepE fill:#E1C1FF,stroke:#333 classDef stepF fill:#FFC1E1,stroke:#333 classDef stepG fill:#D1D1D1,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Lead Configuration Vector Map ```mermaid graph TD I[Lead I: 0 deg
LA to RA]:::leadI II[Lead II: 60 deg
LL to RA]:::leadII III[Lead III: 120 deg
LL to LA]:::leadIII aVR[Aug VR: -150 deg
RA reference]:::avr aVL[Aug VL: -30 deg
LA reference]:::avl aVF[Aug VF: 90 deg
LL reference]:::avf ORIGIN[Frontal Plane
0 deg Reference]:::origin ORIGIN --> I ORIGIN --> II ORIGIN --> III ORIGIN --> aVR ORIGIN --> aVL ORIGIN --> aVF classDef leadI fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 classDef leadII fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 classDef leadIII fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 classDef avr fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 classDef avl fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 classDef avf fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 classDef origin fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.4 ECG Waveform — Time-Domain Schematic ```mermaid graph LR P[P Wave
Atrial Depol
~0.08 s]:::waveP Q[Q
Septal Depol]:::waveQ R[R Peak
Main Ventricular]:::waveR S[S
Late Ventricular]:::waveS T[T Wave
Repolarization
~0.16 s]:::waveT U[U Wave
Optional
Papillary Muscle]:::waveU P --> Q --> R --> S --> T --> U classDef waveP fill:#FF9999,stroke:#333 classDef waveQ fill:#FFCC99,stroke:#333 classDef waveR fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px classDef waveS fill:#CCFF99,stroke:#333 classDef waveT fill:#99FFCC,stroke:#333 classDef waveU fill:#99CCFF,stroke:#333 ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read this diagram for KTU exams:** Each arrow represents a continuous time progression. The R wave is the tallest because ventricular muscle mass (~250–300 g) is much larger than atrial mass (~50 g), producing greater dipole magnitude. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the Einthoven triangle. State Einthoven's law and explain its significance in ECG instrumentation. **Model Answer:** The Einthoven triangle is an imaginary equilateral triangle formed on the frontal plane of the human body, with its three vertices located at the **Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), and Left Leg (LL)** electrodes. The heart's electrical center (equivalent dipole) is assumed to be located at the centroid of this triangle. **Einthoven's Law** states: $$V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$$ This is algebraically derived from the definitions of the three bipolar leads, and hence is an *identity*, not an empirical finding. **Significance in Instrumentation:** 1. Acts as a built-in consistency check — if $V_{II} \neq V_I + V_{III}$, the ECG machine has a calibration fault or electrode displacement. 2. Only two leads need to be physically measured; the third can be computed, reducing hardware by 33%. 3. Provides the mathematical foundation for augmented leads and the 12-lead system. *(Word limit target: ~80 words for 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List the standard ECG waveforms in order and indicate the physiological event each represents. **Model Answer:** | # | Waveform | Physiological Event | |---|---|---| | 1 | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | | 2 | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization (Q = septal, R = main mass, S = basal) | | 3 | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | | 4 | **U wave** (optional) | Repolarization of papillary muscles / Purkinje fibers | The **atrial repolarization (Ta wave)** is normally obscured by the much larger QRS complex. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** (a) **Derive Einthoven's Law from the basic definitions of the three bipolar limb leads.** Show that the relationship $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ is an algebraic identity. **(7 marks)** (b) **Explain the augmented unipolar limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF). Derive the expression for aVR in terms of the bipolar leads $V_I$ and $V_{II}$.** **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Given:** $$V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA} \quad \ldots (1)$$ $$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} \quad \ldots (2)$$ $$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} \quad \ldots (3)$$ **To prove:** $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ **Step-by-step derivation:** Adding equations (1) and (3): $$\begin{aligned} V_I + V_{III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\ &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\ &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\ &= V_{II} \end{aligned}$$ Hence $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ is an algebraic identity. $\blacksquare$ **Discussion (for full marks):** - The relationship holds **regardless** of cardiac electrical activity. - It is independent of dipole orientation or magnitude. - It serves as a **diagnostic tool for electrode integrity** in clinical ECG machines. - A deviation of more than **±0.5 mV** from this identity indicates a faulty lead or poor skin contact. --- **Model Solution for (b):** Augmented unipolar limb leads were introduced by **Emanuel Goldberger (1942)** to overcome the low amplitude (~50%) of Wilson's original unipolar limb leads. **General form:** $$aV_X = V_X - \frac{V_Y + V_Z}{2}$$ where X is the exploring electrode and Y, Z are the other two limb electrodes. **For aVR (X = RA, Y = LA, Z = LL):** $$aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$ **Step 1:** Express $V_{LA}$ and $V_{LL}$ in terms of bipolar leads. From (1): $V_{LA} = V_I + V_{RA}$ From (2): $V_{LL} = V_{II} + V_{RA}$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$\begin{aligned} aVR &= V_{RA} - \frac{(V_I + V_{RA}) + (V_{II} + V_{RA})}{2} \\ &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_I + V_{II} + 2V_{RA}}{2} \\ &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2} - V_{RA} \\ &= -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{aVR = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2}}$$ Similarly, by cyclic permutation: $$aVL = V_I - \frac{V_{II}}{2} \quad ; \quad aVF = V_{II} - \frac{V_I}{2}$$ **Verification:** $aVR + aVL + aVF = 0$ (must hold). --- ### Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** (a) **With a neat diagram, explain the Einthoven triangle. Define each of the three bipolar leads and derive expressions for the cardiac vector components $H_x$ and $H_y$ in terms of $V_I$, $V_{II}$, and $V_{III}$.** **(7 marks)** (b) **The peak QRS amplitudes measured on a patient are $V_I = +1.4$ mV, $V_{II} = +1.8$ mV, and $V_{III} = +0.4$ mV. Compute the mean electrical axis of the heart and state whether it is normal.** **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** [Diagram as shown in Section 4.1 — Einthoven triangle with RA, LA, LL at vertices and cardiac dipole at centroid.] **Definitions:** $$\begin{aligned} V_I &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} \quad (\text{horizontal lead, axis } 0^\circ)\\ V_{II} &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \quad (\text{axis } +60^\circ)\\ V_{III} &= V_{LL} - V_{LA} \quad (\text{axis } +120^\circ) \end{aligned}$$ **Derivation of vector components (already detailed in Section 3.3):** $$\boxed{H_x = \frac{2 V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}{3k} \quad ; \quad H_y = \frac{V_{II} - V_{III}}{k \sqrt{3}}}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $V_I = 1.4$ mV, $V_{II} = 1.8$ mV, $V_{III} = 0.4$ mV. **Step 1:** Verify Einthoven's Law. $$V_I + V_{III} = 1.4 + 0.4 = 1.8 \text{ mV} = V_{II} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 2:** Compute the axis angle. $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(V_{II} - V_{III})}{2V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}\right)$$ Numerator: $\sqrt{3} \times (1.8 - 0.4) = \sqrt{3} \times 1.4 = 2.425$ mV Denominator: $2(1.4) + 1.8 - 0.4 = 2.8 + 1.4 = 4.2$ mV $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2.425}{4.2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.5774) \approx 30^\circ$$ **Step 3:** Interpret. - **Normal QRS axis range:** −30° to +90°. - $\theta = 30°$ lies well within the normal range. - **Conclusion:** The patient has a **normal electrical axis**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify Einthoven's Law** before numerical problems — losing 1–2 marks even when the rest is correct. > 2. **Sign confusion in $aVR$**: The negative sign is essential; omitting it is a common 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Mixing up $H_x$ and $H_y$ formulae** — remember $V_{III} - V_{II}$ vs $V_{II} - V_{III}$ matters for sign of $H_y$. > 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark questions — even a rough sketch of Einthoven triangle earns 1 mark. > 5. **Wrong units**: ECG voltages must be quoted in **mV** (never in raw Volts without prefix conversion). > 6. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$** in the $H_y$ formula — derived from the $60^\circ$ geometry; not optional. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - 📌 **Einthoven's Triangle** has vertices at **RA, LA, LL** and represents the 3 bipolar limb leads. - 📌 **Three bipolar leads**: $V_I$, $V_{II}$, $V_{III}$ — defined as potential differences between limb pairs. - 📌 **Einthoven's Law**: $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ — algebraic identity, used as instrument calibration check. - 📌 **Augmented leads**: $aVR$, $aVL$, $aVF$ — derived from bipolar leads; identity $aVR + aVL + aVF = 0$ always holds. - 📌 **Standard ECG amplitude**: 0.5–5 mV; **Frequency**: 0.05–150 Hz; **Paper speed**: 25 mm/s. - 📌 **ECG waveform order**: P → QRS → T (and sometimes U). - 📌 **P wave** = Atrial depolarization; **QRS** = Ventricular depolarization; **T wave** = Ventricular repolarization. - 📌 **Cardiac vector components**: $H_x = \frac{2V_I + V_{II} - V_{III}}{3k}$, $H_y = \frac{V_{II} - V_{III}}{k\sqrt{3}}$. - 📌 **Mean electrical axis** = $\tan^{-1}(H_y / H_x)$ in degrees; **Normal range**: −30° to +90°. - 📌 **R wave is tallest** because ventricular muscle mass is 5–6× larger than atrial mass. - 📌 **Atrial repolarization (Ta wave)** is hidden inside the QRS complex. - 📌 **Wilson's Central Terminal**: $V_{CT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} \approx 0$ — reference for unipolar leads. - 📌 **Nobel Prize**: Willem Einthoven, 1924, for the string galvanometer and ECG theory.

topics.title: Standard 12-Lead Configurations and ECG Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT861 - BIO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard 12-Lead Configurations and ECG Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG) & 12-Lead System Definition:**
> The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a bioelectric recording of the **electrical activity of the heart** captured at the body surface, representing the time-varying potential differences generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle fibers. The **Standard 12-Lead Configuration** is the universally adopted clinical electrode arrangement that uses **10 physical electrodes** placed on the patient's limbs and chest to derive **12 distinct electrical "views" (leads)** of the heart's electrical vector, providing a near-complete three-dimensional assessment of cardiac function.

In strict KTU biomedical engineering terminology, a **"Lead"** is *not* an electrode wire — it is a **virtual voltage vector** representing the difference in potential between two specified electrode sites, providing a unique directional perspective of the cardiac dipole.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Helicopter Camera" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for Intuitive Understanding:**
> Imagine you are filming a submarine (the heart) underwater using a helicopter above the ocean surface. The submarine has a flashlight (the cardiac electrical vector) that points in different directions as it moves. To understand exactly where the submarine is and what it is doing, you cannot just take **one** photo from above. Instead, you take **12 photographs from 12 different camera angles** around the helicopter. Each photo (a **lead**) shows you a different 2-D projection of the 3-D flashlight vector. By combining all 12 images, you reconstruct a full 3-D understanding of the submarine's orientation, speed, and direction.

In this analogy:
- **Submarine** → Cardiac muscle (myocardium)
- **Flashlight vector** → Heart's instantaneous electrical dipole moment
- **12 Camera angles** → 12 ECG leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6)
- **Helicopter cables** → The 10 electrode wires connected to the body

## 1.3 The Two Categories of Leads

The 12 leads are divided into **two functional families**:

| Category | Number of Leads | Electrodes Used | View Provided |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Limb Leads (Frontal Plane)** | 6 | 4 limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL, RL) | Vertical (Frontal) plane of the heart |
| **Precordial Leads (Horizontal Plane)** | 6 | 6 chest electrodes + 4 limb electrodes | Horizontal (Transverse) plane of the heart |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Color Code (IEC/AHA Convention):**
> - **Right Arm (RA)** → **Red**
> - **Left Arm (LA)** → **Yellow**
> - **Left Leg (LL)** → **Green**
> - **Right Leg (RL)** → **Black** (this is the *ground/reference* electrode)

---

## 1.4 Sub-Categorization of the 6 Frontal Plane Limb Leads

> [!NOTE]
> The 6 limb leads are themselves split into two families:

**(a) Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Triangle) — 3 leads:**
- **Lead I**, **Lead II**, **Lead III**
- Measured as the *direct potential difference* between two limb electrodes.

**(b) Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger's Leads) — 3 leads:**
- **aVR**, **aVL**, **aVF**
- "Augmented" because the signal is electronically amplified (gain ≈ 1.5×) since the raw unipolar signal would otherwise be too small.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard ECG Metrics (memorize these):**
> - **ECG signal amplitude:** **0.5 mV to 5 mV** (typical ≈ **1 mV** calibration pulse)
> - **Frequency content:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (clinical diagnostic band)
> - **Diagnostic bandwidth:** **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz** (most clinical information)
> - **Standard paper speed:** **25 mm/s**
> - **Standard calibration:** **10 mm/mV** (vertical sensitivity)
> - **Skin-electrode impedance:** must be **< 5 kΩ** (IEC 60601-2-25 standard)
> - **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio):** **> 100 dB** (medical-grade requirement)
> - **ADC Sampling Rate:** ≥ **500 Hz** (Nyquist ≥ 2× highest freq of interest)
> - **ADC Resolution:** **12 to 24 bits**
- **Patient leakage current:** **< 10 µA** (Type CF, cardiac-protected) per IEC 60601-1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Einthoven's Triangle — The Bipolar Limb Leads

In 1903, **Willem Einthoven** (Nobel Laureate, 1924) formalized the frontal-plane limb leads. He conceptualized the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) as forming an **equilateral triangle** around the torso, with the heart at its electrical center. This is the famous **Einthoven's Triangle**.

### Lead Equations (Bipolar):

$$\text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$\text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$\text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

where $V_{RA}$, $V_{LA}$, $V_{LL}$ are the potentials at the right arm, left arm, and left leg electrodes, respectively. The **Right Leg (RL)** electrode is the *common reference / ground* and is not part of any measuring dipole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bipolar?** Because each lead measures a *real* potential difference between two *real* physical electrode sites.

## 2.2 Einthoven's Law (The Cardinal Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Einthoven's Law:**
> $$\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$$
> At any instant in time, the algebraic sum of the potentials in Leads I and III equals the potential in Lead II. This is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** applied to the closed loop of Einthoven's triangle.

### Derivation Logic:
- Apply KVL: $V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$
- LHS = (Lead I) + (Lead III) = RHS = Lead II ✓

## 2.3 Goldberger's Augmented Unipolar Leads

A **unipolar** lead measures the potential at one *exploring* electrode relative to a **composite reference terminal** formed by combining two or more other electrodes.

For **Goldberger's augmented leads**, the reference is a modified **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)** where the exploring electrode itself is *excluded* from the reference average. This exclusion causes the signal to be smaller, so it is **augmented by a factor of 1.5** electronically.

### Lead Equations (Augmented Unipolar):

$$\text{aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{(V_{LA} + V_{LL})}{2}$$

$$\text{aVL} = V_{LA} - \frac{(V_{RA} + V_{LL})}{2}$$

$$\text{aVF} = V_{LL} - \frac{(V_{RA} + V_{LA})}{2}$$

The "V" stands for **Vector**, and the suffixes mean:
- **R** → Right arm
- **L** → Left arm
- **F** → Foot (left leg)

## 2.4 Wilson's Precordial (Precordial = Pre-cordial = In front of the heart) Leads

The **6 precordial leads (V1 to V6)** are placed directly on the chest at anatomically defined intercostal positions:

| Lead | Position (Anatomical Landmark) |
|------|-------------------------------|
| **V1** | 4th intercostal space, **right** sternal border |
| **V2** | 4th intercostal space, **left** sternal border |
| **V3** | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| **V4** | 5th intercostal space, **mid-clavicular line** |
| **V5** | Same horizontal level as V4, **anterior axillary line** |
| **V6** | Same horizontal level as V4, **mid-axillary line** |

Each precordial lead measures the chest electrode potential against the **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)**:

$$V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

$$V_n = V_{V_n} - V_{WCT}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$$

## 2.5 Hexaxial Reference System (Frontal Plane)

The 6 limb leads, when plotted by their angles of view, form the **hexaxial reference system**:

| Lead | Angle (Degrees) |
|------|-----------------|
| Lead I | 0° |
| Lead II | +60° |
| Lead III | +120° |
| aVR | −150° |
| aVL | −30° |
| aVF | +90° |

Each lead is separated by exactly **30°**, providing 6 angular views spanning **360°** around the frontal plane.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU University Exams:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Key Variable / Unit |
|---|------------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Einthoven's Law | $II = I + III$ | Potentials (mV) |
| 2 | Lead I | $I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ | mV |
| 3 | Lead II | $II = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ | mV |
| 4 | Lead III | $III = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ | mV |
| 5 | Wilson Central Terminal | $V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{3}$ | mV |
| 6 | Augmented lead aVR | $aVR = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{2}$ | mV |
| 7 | Augmented lead aVL | $aVL = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LL}}{2}$ | mV |
| 8 | Augmented lead aVF | $aVF = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}}{2}$ | mV |
| 9 | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB |
| 10 | Sampling Frequency (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz (use ≥ 500 Hz) |
| 11 | Heart Rate from ECG | $\text{HR} = \frac{1500}{\text{RR intervals in mm}}$ | bpm (paper 25 mm/s) |
| 12 | ECG Calibration Pulse | $1\text{ mV} \equiv 10\text{ mm}$ | Vertical sensitivity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the `|` symbol in your exam answer tables.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation. This is a common markdown-breaking mistake in KTU digital submissions.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 12-lead ECG is the **single most performed diagnostic test in modern medicine** (> 300 million ECGs/year worldwide). Its engineering utility extends to:
- **Myocardial Infarction (MI) localization** — ST-elevation in specific leads pinpoints which coronary artery is blocked.
- **Arrhythmia detection** — irregular RR intervals.
- **Electrolyte imbalance screening** — peaked T waves (hyperkalemia), prolonged QT (hypocalcemia).
- **Cardiac chamber enlargement** — P-wave morphology, QRS widening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Einthoven's Law

We will derive the fundamental relationship $II = I + III$ from first principles using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.

**Starting Point:**
Recall the definitions of the three bipolar leads as potential differences between limb electrodes:

$$ \text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA} $$

$$ \text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} $$

$$ \text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} $$

**Step 1 — Algebraic Sum of Lead I and Lead III:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\
&= V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\
&= V_{LL} - V_{RA}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The $V_{LA}$ terms cancel out because they appear with opposite signs.

**Step 2 — Compare with Lead II Definition:**

From the definition: $\text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$

By inspection, the result of Step 1 is **identical** to Lead II.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$\therefore \quad \text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}$$

This is the **mathematical proof of Einthoven's Law**. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** This law means that only **two** of the three bipolar leads are *linearly independent*. The third is redundant. This is also why ECG machines internally compute one lead from the other two as a built-in **self-consistency check** — if the displayed trace violates this law, the machine flags a lead-fail error.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Augmented Lead aVR

We derive the augmented unipolar lead aVR, which measures the potential at the Right Arm with reference to a "central terminal" formed by *averaging* the Left Arm and Left Leg potentials (note: RA is *excluded* from this average, unlike WCT).

**Starting Point:**
Define the Goldberger central terminal for aVR as:

$$V_{CT,aVR} = \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Define Raw Unipolar Signal:**

The unipolar signal (before augmentation) is:

$$V_{aVR,\text{raw}} = V_{RA} - V_{CT,aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Augmentation Factor:**
Goldberger observed that excluding RA from the reference reduces the signal magnitude. The augmentation factor compensates for this. The mathematical justification:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{WCT} &= \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} \\
V_{CT,aVR} &= \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{CT,aVR}}{V_{WCT}} &= \frac{(V_{LA}+V_{LL})/2}{(V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL})/3} \\
&= \frac{3(V_{LA}+V_{LL})}{2(V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL})}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* If we assume the body is a roughly symmetric volume conductor and $V_{RA} \approx -(V_{LA}+V_{LL})$ (Kirchhoff constraint on body potential), then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{CT,aVR}}{V_{WCT}} &\approx \frac{3(V_{LA}+V_{LL})}{2 \cdot 0} \quad \text{(form undefined, but ratio of magnitudes } \to \frac{3}{2})
\end{aligned}$$

The rigorously derived factor is **3/2 = 1.5**.

**Step 4 — Final Augmented Equation:**

$$aVR = \frac{3}{2} \cdot V_{aVR,\text{raw}} = \frac{3}{2}\left[V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}\right]$$

This is the standard KTU formula appearing in textbooks. ∎

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lead Computation Table

Below is a worked example. Assume the following electrode potentials (in mV) measured at one instant during the cardiac cycle:

- $V_{RA} = +0.20$ mV
- $V_{LA} = +0.80$ mV
- $V_{LL} = +0.50$ mV

**Compute all 6 limb leads:**

| Lead | Formula | Substitution | Value (mV) |
|------|---------|--------------|-----------|
| **I** | $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ | $0.80 - 0.20$ | **+0.60** |
| **II** | $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ | $0.50 - 0.20$ | **+0.30** |
| **III** | $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ | $0.50 - 0.80$ | **−0.30** |
| **Verify Law** | $I + III$ | $0.60 + (-0.30)$ | **+0.30 = II ✓** |
| **aVR** | $V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{2}$ | $0.20 - \frac{1.30}{2}$ | **−0.45** |
| **aVL** | $V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LL}}{2}$ | $0.80 - \frac{0.70}{2}$ | **+0.45** |
| **aVF** | $V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}}{2}$ | $0.50 - \frac{1.00}{2}$ | **0.00** |
| **Verify Sum** | $aVR + aVL + aVF$ | $-0.45 + 0.45 + 0.00$ | **0.00 ✓** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Invariant (KCL-like rule for augmented leads):**
> $$aVR + aVL + aVF = 0$$
> This is the augmented-lead equivalent of Einthoven's Law, and is also a built-in self-check used by diagnostic ECG machines.

## 3.4 Heart Rate Calculation from RR Interval

Given the standard paper speed of 25 mm/s, the time-axis conversion factor is:

$$1 \text{ mm (horizontal)} = \frac{1}{25} \text{ s} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

Therefore, the heart rate in beats per minute (bpm) given an RR interval of $N$ mm is:

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{(N \times 0.04) \text{ s/beat}} = \frac{1500}{N} \text{ bpm}$$

**Example:** If the RR interval measures 20 mm on standard ECG paper, then:

$$\text{HR} = \frac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$

This is the famous "**1500 rule**" for ECG heart rate calculation. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 ECG Machine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA1["Patient Body Surface"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows (→)** = forward signal flow.
> - **Dotted arrows (-.->)** = auxiliary/protection/feedback paths.
> - The **Right Leg Drive (RLD)** circuit actively *injects* a phase-inverted common-mode signal back into the RL electrode to cancel out mains interference (50/60 Hz). This dramatically improves the effective **CMRR**.

## 4.3 Lead Configuration Summary Table

| Family | Lead | Type | Reference Electrode | Exploring Electrode | View Angle |

| Limb | I | Bipolar | RA | LA | 0° |
| Limb | II | Bipolar | RA | LL | +60° |
