# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. What is Einthoven's Triangle? Explain the bipolar limb leads with the help of a neat diagram. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Einthoven's Triangle, proposed by **Willem Einthoven** in 1903, is an equilateral triangle formed by the three limb electrodes — Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), and Left Leg (LL) — with the heart at its electrical center. The Right Leg electrode serves as a ground reference and is not part of the triangle.

The three **bipolar limb leads** measure the potential difference between two limb electrodes:

$$\text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$\text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$\text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

Each lead provides a different electrical "view" of the heart in the frontal plane, and together they form the basis of standard ECG interpretation.

*(Examiner Note: A student drawing the triangle without labeling the leads and their angles loses up to 1 mark.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between unipolar and bipolar ECG leads. Give two examples of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bipolar Leads | Unipolar Leads |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Measure potential between *two* exploring electrodes | Measure potential at *one* exploring electrode vs. a *composite reference* |
| **Signal magnitude** | Larger (no augmentation needed) | Smaller; often augmented electronically (1.5× gain) |
| **Examples** | Lead I, Lead II, Lead III | aVR, aVL, aVF (augmented limb); V1–V6 (precordial) |
| **Reference** | Specific limb electrode | Wilson Central Terminal (or modified WCT for Goldberger) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module 2, Sample Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a standard ECG machine. List the functions of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A standard ECG machine acquires, amplifies, filters, digitizes, and displays the cardiac bioelectric signal. The block diagram is as follows:

1. **Electrodes (10 nos.)** — Ag/AgCl disposable electrodes placed on limbs (RA, LA, LL, RL) and chest (V1–V6). Convert ionic skin currents into electronic currents.

2. **Lead Selector Network** — A multiplexer that sequentially connects the 12 lead configurations to the amplifier.

3. **Defibrillation Protection Circuit** — High-voltage surge protection (clamps at ±30 V) to safeguard the instrument if defibrillation is applied.

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — A three-op-amp topology providing differential gain of ≈ 1000 (60 dB), high input impedance (> 10 MΩ), and high CMRR (> 100 dB).

5. **Isolation Barrier (Optical or Transformer)** — Galvanically isolates the patient side from the mains-powered circuitry, limiting leakage current to < 10 µA (Type CF, cardiac floating).

6. **Analog Filter Stage** — A cascade of:
   - **High-pass filter** at **0.05 Hz** (removes DC baseline drift from electrode half-cell potentials),
   - **Low-pass filter** at **150 Hz** (limits noise bandwidth),
   - **Notch filter** at **50 Hz** (rejects India/Europe mains interference).

7. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Samples the signal at ≥ **500 Hz** with **12–24 bit resolution**.

8. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — Performs digital filtering, baseline correction, beat detection, and HR calculation.

9. **Display & Storage** — LCD screen showing 12-lead traces (typically 3×4 or 6×2 layout), with thermal printer output and on-board memory/SD storage.

**(b) Explain the Standard 12-Lead ECG configuration. State the positions of the precordial electrodes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Standard 12-Lead ECG uses **10 physical electrodes** to derive **12 leads**:

**Limb Leads (Frontal Plane, 6 leads):**
- **3 Bipolar (Einthoven):** Lead I, II, III
- **3 Augmented Unipolar (Goldberger):** aVR, aVL, aVF

**Precordial Leads (Horizontal Plane, 6 leads):** V1 to V6, positioned at:
- **V1:** 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
- **V2:** 4th intercostal space, left sternal border
- **V3:** Midway between V2 and V4
- **V4:** 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line
- **V5:** Same level as V4, anterior axillary line
- **V6:** Same level as V4, mid-axillary line

Each precordial lead uses the **Wilson Central Terminal** $\left(V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{3}\right)$ as the indifferent electrode.

**Einthoven's Law:** $II = I + III$, a built-in consistency check.

**Hexaxial Reference System:** The 6 limb leads view the heart from 6 angles spaced 30° apart, covering 360°.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**(a) Derive the relationship between the three bipolar limb leads (Einthoven's Law). Show that only two of the three leads are linearly independent. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting with the definitions:
- $\text{Lead I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$
- $\text{Lead II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$
- $\text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$

Adding Lead I and Lead III:

$$\begin{aligned}
I + III &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\
&= V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\
&= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\
&= \text{Lead II}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $II = I + III$, which is **Einthoven's Law**.

Since Lead III can be obtained as $III = II - I$, the three bipolar leads have only **two degrees of freedom** in the frontal plane — i.e., they span a 2-D vector space. Hence, only **two** of the three are linearly independent. ∎

**(b) Explain the principle of augmented unipolar limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF) as given by Goldberger. Why is the augmentation factor 1.5? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A unipolar lead measures the potential at one "exploring" electrode against a composite central reference. Goldberger's modification **excludes** the exploring electrode from the reference terminal:

- For **aVR**: Reference = $\frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$ (RA excluded)
- For **aVL**: Reference = $\frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}$ (LA excluded)
- For **aVF**: Reference = $\frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}$ (LL excluded)

Excluding the exploring electrode causes the measured signal to be smaller. Goldberger showed that, assuming the body's volume conductor and applying Kirchhoff's current law on the body trunk (which forces $V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL} \approx 0$ as a DC reference), the ratio of the modified reference to the standard Wilson Central Terminal is exactly **3/2 = 1.5**.

Hence, the raw unipolar signal is electronically **augmented by 1.5×** to restore its magnitude, giving the **aV** prefix (augmented vector). ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Lead" with "Electrode":** A lead is *not* a wire; it is a *voltage vector*. This conceptual error alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the color code (IEC/AHA):** Examiners often ask "What is the color of the Right Leg electrode?" → **Black** (ground). Many students incorrectly write "Green."
> 3. **Missing the RL electrode's role:** Students often forget that RL is *not* part of Einthoven's triangle — it is purely a driven ground reference for patient safety.
> 4. **Saying "10 leads" instead of "12 leads":** The system has 10 electrodes but produces 12 *leads*.
> 5. **Skipping the augmentation factor:** Many students write the Goldberger equations *without* the 1.5× factor, costing 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing WCT with Goldberger references:** Wilson uses *all three* limb electrodes averaged; Goldberger uses *two* (excluding the exploring one).
> 7. **Not mentioning patient safety standards (IEC 60601-1, Type CF)** in the ECG machine block diagram — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔖 Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **10 electrodes → 12 leads** (not the other way around).
- [ ] **6 limb leads** (frontal plane) + **6 precordial leads** (horizontal plane).
- [ ] **Einthoven's Law:** $II = I + III$ — fundamental invariant.
- [ ] **Goldberger's invariant:** $aVR + aVL + aVF = 0$ — second invariant.
- [ ] **Color Code (IEC):** RA = **Red**, LA = **Yellow**, LL = **Green**, RL = **Black (Ground)**.
- [ ] **Augmentation factor** = 1.5 (3/2) for aVR, aVL, aVF.
- [ ] **WCT (Wilson Central Terminal)** = average of $V_{RA}$, $V_{LA}$, $V_{LL}$.
- [ ] **Standard calibration:** 1 mV = 10 mm vertical, paper speed = 25 mm/s.
- [ ] **HR rule:** HR = $\frac{1500}{\text{RR interval in mm}}$ (the "1500 rule").
- [ ] **CMRR** > 100 dB; **Bandwidth** = 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz; **Leakage current** < 10 µA.
- [ ] **Sampling rate** ≥ 500 Hz (Nyquist criterion for 150 Hz bandwidth).
- [ ] **Patient safety:** IEC 60601-1, Type CF, optical/transformer isolation.
- [ ] **V1–V6 positions:** Memorize the 4th and 5th intercostal space landmarks.
- [ ] **Hexaxial angles:** Lead I = 0°, Lead II = +60°, aVF = +90°, Lead III = +120°, aVR = −150°, aVL = −30° (separated by 30°).
- [ ] **Right Leg Drive (RLD)** = active common-mode feedback for 50 Hz interference rejection.
- [ ] **Defibrillation protection** clamp at ≈ ±30 V is mandatory in ECG input stages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroencephalography (EEG) and Brain Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT861 - BIO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electroencephalography (EEG) and Brain Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the electrical activity of the brain. It employs an array of surface electrodes (or, in specialized cases, invasive depth electrodes) placed on the scalp to detect the summed post-synaptic potential fluctuations of cortical pyramidal neurons, measured as time-varying voltage differences in the range of **5 µV to 300 µV** over a dominant frequency spectrum of **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz** (clinical band typically 0.5–30 Hz).

The resultant time-series signal is called an **electroencephalogram**. The origin of clinical EEG is credited to **Hans Berger (1929)**, who first demonstrated that rhythmic electrical activity could be recorded non-invasively from the human scalp.

In the KTU 2024 Bio-Mechatronics framework, the EEG system is treated as a **bio-electric acquisition front-end** comprising electrodes, a high-gain differential amplifier, filters, an analog-to-digital converter (ADC), and a graphical display/recording unit.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Student Intuition

Imagine the brain as a **vast electrical orchestra** with billions of neurons (musicians) playing at any instant. We cannot put a microphone inside the skull (concert hall) without cracking it open. So we glue small microphones (**scalp electrodes**) to the outside walls of the hall. Each microphone picks up the *combined*, *attenuated*, and *delayed* sound wave that leaks through the wall.

- A *single neuron firing* = one clap. → Too weak to hear.
- *Thousands of pyramidal neurons firing synchronously* = a sustained chord. → Detectable outside.
- The chord's pitch (frequency) tells us the *mental state*: deep sleep, relaxation, alertness, anxiety.
- The chord's volume (amplitude) tells us *how synchronised* the neurons are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pyramidal neurons?** They are aligned perpendicularly to the cortical surface, like parallel dipoles. When they fire together, their dipole fields add constructively — this *summation* is what makes EEG possible. Other randomly oriented neurons cancel each other out.

The output — a wavy line on a screen — is the **Brain Wave**, and its dominant frequency band classifies the *state of consciousness* of the subject.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Symbol / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| EEG Signal Amplitude | **5 – 300 µV** | microvolts |
| Clinical Frequency Band | **0.5 – 30 Hz** | Hertz |
| Full EEG Bandwidth (incl. high-freq) | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | Hertz |
| Sampling Rate (clinical) | ≥ **256 Hz** | samples/sec |
| ADC Resolution | ≥ **12-bit** (preferred 16-bit) | bits |
| Number of Channels | 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256 | channels |
| Skin–Electrode Impedance | < **5 kΩ** | kilo-ohms |
| Amplifier Input Impedance | > **10 MΩ** | mega-ohms |
| Amplifier Common Mode Rejection Ratio | > **80 dB** (clinical: ≥ 100 dB) | decibels |
| Total System Noise | < **2 µV (peak-to-peak)** | microvolts |

These values are mandated by the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)** and the **American Clinical Neurophysiology Society (ACNS)** standards, which align with KTU's biomedical instrumentation lab guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Neurophysiological Origin of EEG Signal

### Step 1 — The Cellular Source
EEG signals are NOT generated by individual action potentials. They are produced by **Excitatory Post-Synaptic Potentials (EPSPs)** and **Inhibitory Post-Synaptic Potentials (IPSPs)** occurring in the **apical dendrites of cortical pyramidal neurons** (layers III, V of the 6-layered neocortex).

### Step 2 — Why Synchronisation Matters
- Single neuron postsynaptic potential: ≈ 1 mV inside the dendrite.
- Volume-conducted to scalp: attenuated by ≈ 1000× → ≈ 1 µV.
- For the scalp electrode to "see" anything, **~10,000 to 50,000 neurons must fire synchronously**, with their dipoles oriented in parallel (which is exactly what pyramidal cells do).

### Step 3 — Volume Conduction
The skull and scalp act as a **low-pass spatial filter**, smoothing and attenuating high-frequency spatial detail. This is why EEG has excellent *temporal* resolution (millisecond scale) but poor *spatial* resolution (~2–3 cm at scalp, vs. ~1 mm for fMRI).

---

## 2.2 Electrode Types and Montages

### Electrodes
1. **Passive (Ag/AgCl) Disc Electrodes** — most common; uses conductive gel to lower skin-electrode impedance.
2. **Active Electrodes** — incorporate a preamplifier at the electrode site to reduce cable noise.
3. **Dry Electrodes** — emerging; no gel needed, but higher impedance (~50–500 kΩ).
4. **Needle/Sphenoidal Electrodes** — invasive, used in pre-surgical epilepsy evaluation.
5. **Depth Electrodes** — intracerebral, used in epilepsy surgery mapping.

### Montages (Electrode Pairing Strategies)
- **Unipolar / Referential Montage**: Each active electrode is referenced to a single common reference (e.g., linked ears, A1+A2). Best for *localisation*.
- **Bipolar Montage**: Difference between two adjacent active electrodes. Best for *cancellation of common noise* and identification of phase reversals.
- **Average Reference Montage**: Reference = mean of all electrodes. Reduces bias.

---

## 2.3 EEG Acquisition System — Block-Level Architecture

A clinical EEG chain consists of:

```
Scalp → Electrodes → Pre-amplifier → Isolation Amp (patient safety) 
      → High-pass & Low-pass Filters → Main Amplifier (Gain ~10,000)
      → Notch Filter (50/60 Hz line noise) → ADC → Display/Storage
```

### Critical Specifications
- **Differential Amplifier Gain**: ~10,000 (to lift 100 µV signal to ~1 V ADC range).
- **Input Impedance**: > **10¹² Ω** (FET-input op-amps) so as not to load the high-impedance electrode–skin interface.
- **CMRR**: > **100 dB** at 50 Hz to reject mains interference.
- **Notch Filter**: Twin-T or digital IIR, Q ≈ 30, centred at 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (US).

---

## 2.4 Brain Wave Classification

The dominant frequency of the EEG signal correlates with the behavioural state. KTU examiners frequently test this table.

| Wave | Frequency Band | Typical Amplitude | Physiological / Cognitive State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | 20 – 200 µV | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants, pathological coma |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | < 30 µV | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, memory encoding |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | 20 – 60 µV | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, occipital dominance |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | < 20 µV | Active thinking, alert concentration, anxiety |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz (up to 100 Hz) | very low (< 5 µV) | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding, feature integration |
| **Mu (µ)** | 8 – 13 Hz (alpha variant) | ~50 µV | Sensorimotor cortex idle rhythm; suppressed by movement |
| **Lambda (λ)** | sharp transients | – | Visual processing of scanned images |

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Pearl**: When alpha rhythm is suppressed by opening the eyes, this is called **"Alpha Blocking"** or the **Berger Effect**. This is a routinely tested viva question.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$ | Differential amplifier output (instrumentation amp core) |
| 2 | $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Rejection of common-mode (e.g., mains) noise |
| 3 | $A_d = \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$ | Three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier gain |
| 4 | $V_{n,\text{in}} < 2\ \mu V_{pp}$ | Noise floor requirement for EEG |
| 5 | $f_{HP} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | High-pass filter (typically 0.5 Hz) cutoff |
| 6 | $f_{LP} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Low-pass filter (typically 30–70 Hz) cutoff |
| 7 | $f_{notch} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | 50 Hz twin-T notch filter |
| 8 | $Z_{in} > 100\cdot Z_{electrode}$ | Rule-of-thumb to avoid signal loading |
| 9 | $\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| 10 | $f_{s,\min} = 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion |
| 11 | $L = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits}} \cdot V_{\text{range}}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Quantisation noise RMS |
| 12 | $\text{Position}_{10\text{-}20} = \dfrac{10\%\ \text{or}\ 20\%}{100\%}\cdot D$ | 10-20 system electrode placement rule |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Clinical Diagnostics**: Epilepsy (spike-and-wave, polyspike), sleep disorders, encephalopathies, brain death confirmation, stroke localisation.
2. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)**: P300 speller, motor imagery BCIs use EEG mu/beta rhythm desynchronisation (ERD/ERS).
3. **Neurofeedback & Cognitive Engineering**: Real-time alpha/theta training for anxiety, ADHD, peak-performance training.
4. **Anaesthesia Depth Monitoring**: BIS (Bispectral Index) is derived from EEG.
5. **Biorobotics**: EEG-controlled wheelchairs, prosthetic limbs in advanced rehab mechatronics.
6. **Consumer Wearables**: Emotiv, Muse, Neurosity — mechatronic integration of dry-electrode EEG.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exam-grade**. Each algebraic transition is explained. Master these to score full marks in 14-mark derivations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of EEG Differential Amplifier

### Background
The scalp EEG signal sits on top of a much larger **common-mode voltage**, primarily the 50 Hz mains interference capacitively coupled into the body. To extract the differential signal, we use a **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** (commonly the AD620 / INA128).

### Step 1 — Define the Two Gain Components

The amplifier responds to two input components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= V_1 - V_2 &&\text{(differential signal, e.g., a real EEG wave)}\\[2pt]
V_{cm} &= \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} &&\text{(common-mode voltage, e.g., 50 Hz pickup)}\\[2pt]
V_{out} &= A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $A_d$ is the differential gain (ideally large, $\sim 10^3$) and $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain (ideally zero, $\sim 10^{-1}$).

### Step 2 — Define CMRR Numerically

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR} &= \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Decibels (Engineering Convention)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Standard)
Suppose a clinical EEG amp uses AD620 with $A_d = 1000$ and $A_{cm} = 0.01$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR} &= \frac{1000}{0.01} = 100\,000\\[2pt]
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} &= 20 \log_{10}(100\,000)\\[2pt]
&= 20 \times 5 = 100\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: A 100 dB CMRR means a 1 V common-mode mains interference is attenuated to **10 µV at the output**, comparable in magnitude to the EEG signal itself. Hence high CMRR is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

This is the workhorse of every clinical EEG front-end.

### Circuit Operation
The first stage (two op-amps A1, A2) provides differential gain set by a single resistor $R_g$ (gain-set). The second stage (op-amp A3) is a unity-gain difference amplifier that rejects common-mode.

### Step 1 — Output of First Stage

If $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the two inputs, $R$ is the feedback resistor, and $R_g$ is the gain resistor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out1} - V_{out2} &= \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Output of Difference Stage

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{R_f}{R}\,(V_{out1} - V_{out2})\\[4pt]
        &= \frac{R_f}{R}\left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Overall Differential Gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_d &= \frac{R_f}{R}\left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_g}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the popular AD620 IC, $\frac{R_f}{R} = 1$, simplifying to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_d &= 1 + \frac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Choose $R_g$ for EEG Gain of 10,000

$$
\begin{aligned}
10\,000 &= 1 + \frac{49\,400}{R_g}\\[4pt]
9\,999 &= \frac{49\,400}{R_g}\\[4pt]
R_g   &= \frac{49\,400}{9\,999}\ \Omega \approx 4.94\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Design Insight**: Such a tiny $R_g$ makes the gain very sensitive to resistor tolerance. This is why **precision resistors** (±0.1 %) or **laser-trimmed ICs** (like AD620) are mandatory in clinical EEG.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The 10-20 International Electrode Placement System

### Background
To achieve reproducible EEG measurements across patients and labs, **Jasper (1958)** standardised the **10-20 system**. Electrode positions are at **10 % or 20 %** of measured skull distances.

### Step 1 — Define the Reference Distances
- $D_{AP}$ = anterior–posterior distance = inion (back of head) to nasion (top of nose bridge).
- $D_{LR}$ = left pre-auricular point to right pre-auricular point (ear tragus to ear tragus across the top).

### Step 2 — Compute the Unit Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{AP} &= \frac{D_{AP}}{10}\\[2pt]
U_{LR} &= \frac{D_{LR}}{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Locate Electrodes at Multiples

Each key electrode (Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, Fz, Cz, Pz) sits at multiples of 10 % or 20 % from these reference points.

For example, the central midline electrode $Cz$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position}_{Cz} &= \text{Midpoint of }D_{AP} = \frac{D_{AP}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Percentage-Based Formula

For any electrode offset $k$ along the anteroposterior line from vertex:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \left(\frac{k}{100}\right) \cdot D_{AP},\quad k \in \{10,\, 20,\, 20,\, 20,\, 20,\, 10\}
\end{aligned}
$$

(These percentages add to 100 %, traversing Fp → Fz → Cz → Pz → O along the midline.)

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
Suppose $D_{AP} = 32$ cm for a patient.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{AP} &= 3.2\ \text{cm}\\[2pt]
\text{Distance from Fpz to Fz} &= 2 \cdot U_{AP} = 6.4\ \text{cm}\\[2pt]
\text{Distance from Fpz to Cz} &= 5 \cdot U_{AP} = 16\ \text{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why 10-20?** Using 10 % and 20 % of head dimensions *normalises* electrode positions across head sizes, enabling cross-subject comparison.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quantisation Noise and Minimum ADC Bits

### Step 1 — Quantisation Step Size
For an ADC with $N$ bits covering input range $\pm V_{ref}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \frac{2 V_{ref}}{2^N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — RMS Quantisation Noise

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{q,\text{rms}} &= \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N-1}\sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Choose N for EEG
Let $V_{ref} = 5$ V (post-amplification), and require $V_{q,\text{rms}} < 1\ \mu V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{-6} &= \frac{5}{2^{N-1}\sqrt{3}}\\[4pt]
2^{N-1} &= \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{10^{-6}} \approx 8.66 \times 10^{6}\\[4pt]
N - 1 &= \log_2(8.66 \times 10^6) \approx 23.05\\[4pt]
N &\approx 24.05
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion**: A 24-bit ADC (e.g., ADS1299 used in OpenBCI) is needed for full-resolution digital EEG. **16-bit** is acceptable only after sufficient analog gain (e.g., 10,000×) compresses the dynamic range so that 1 LSB ≈ 0.15 µV.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Skin-Electrode Half-Cell Potential

The Ag/AgCl electrode develops a half-cell potential $E_{Ag/AgCl} \approx +0.223\ \text{V}$ versus SHE. In a differential measurement, electrode mismatches create a **DC offset** of up to several hundred mV that must be blocked by the high-pass filter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{offset,DC}} &= E_1 - E_2 \approx \pm 50\ \text{mV (typical mismatch)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The high-pass filter with $f_{HP} = 0.5$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{HP} &= \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \implies RC = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 0.5} \approx 0.318\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This filter attenuates the DC offset by a factor of $\geq 1000$ while preserving EEG frequencies.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Speed-Revision Summary

| Parameter | Formula | Typical Numerical Value | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amplifier Gain | $A_d = 1 + 2R/R_g$ | ~10,000 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| CMRR | $20\log(A_d/A_{cm})$ | 100 dB | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10-20 Position | $k\% \cdot D$ | 10 %, 20 % | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | ≥ 256 Hz | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| ADC Bits | $N = \lceil \log_2(V_{ref}/V_{q,\min}\sqrt{3}) \rceil$ | 16–24 bit | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| HPF cutoff | $1/(2\pi RC)$ | 0.5 Hz | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Notch $f_0$ | $1/(2\pi RC)$ | 50 Hz | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
