topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT861 - BIO MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sleep Stages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SLEEP STAGES: CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sleep Stage** is a clinically and bioelectrically defined epoch of sleep characterized by a distinct, measurable combination of **electroencephalographic (EEG)**, **electro-oculographic (EOG)**, and **electromyographic (EMG)** signatures. According to the **American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) Scoring Manual**, a normal adult sleep episode is partitioned into two overarching physiologies — **Non-Rapid Eye Movement (NREM)** and **Rapid Eye Movement (REM)** — with NREM further sub-classified into three stages: **N1 (NREM-1)**, **N2 (NREM-2)**, and **N3 (NREM-3 / Slow-Wave Sleep)**.

In the **Biomechatronics** domain, sleep staging is not merely a clinical categorization — it is the **engineering signal-processing backbone** that drives automated sleep monitors, polysomnographs (PSG), actigraphy devices, closed-loop neuromodulation implants (e.g., for sleep apnea or insomnia), and **AI-based wearable sleep classifiers**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sleep Theatre"

Imagine your brain as a **grand theatre** performing a five-act play every single night:

- **Act I (Awake/Stage W)** → The stage lights are fully on; actors (neurons) are chatting loudly with the audience.
- **Act II (Stage N1)** → Dimmer lights; the actors whisper — the brain begins to "drift."
- **Act III (Stage N2)** → Lights off for the audience; only the stage crew is visible — **memory filing** is happening backstage.
- **Act IV (Stage N3)** → Deep blackout; the actors are now in deep maintenance — tissues are repaired, hormones are secreted.
- **Act V (Stage R / REM)** → A surreal dream-show begins — eyes dart, body is paralyzed, mind is hyperactive.

Each "act" corresponds to a unique **electrical symphony** that electrodes on the scalp, around the eyes, and on the chin can record.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Biomechatronics

| Engineering Domain | Direct Application of Sleep Staging |
|---|---|
| **Medical Device Design** | PSG hardware, EEG amplifier design |
| **Signal Processing** | Frequency-band filtering, spectral analysis |
| **Embedded Systems** | Real-time sleep classifiers on MCUs |
| **Wearable Tech** | Smart bands, smart-watches, EEG headbands |
| **Neural Engineering** | Closed-loop vagus/pons stimulators |
| **AI / ML** | CNN/LSTM-based automated sleep scoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sleep Staging is the foundational knowledge** required to design **any** biomedical system that interacts with the central nervous system during rest. Without it, devices like CPAP machines, sleep-tracking rings, and neuromodulators cannot be properly engineered or clinically validated.

## 1.4 Standard Physiological Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard EEG Sampling Rate:** **256 Hz** (clinical PSG) or **200 Hz** (wearables)
- **Scoring Epoch Length:** **30 seconds** (the universal "page" of sleep)
- **Total Nightly Sleep Cycles:** **4–6 cycles** per 8-hour sleep
- **Cycle Duration:** approximately **90 minutes** each

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The AASM Sleep Stage Taxonomy (Authoritative)

The current gold-standard scoring rule (AASM, 2017 update) defines sleep using **3 signals**: EEG (central + occipital), EOG (left & right), and chin EMG.

| Stage | Old Name (R&K) | EEG Dominant Frequency | Defining Feature | Typical % of TST |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **W (Wake)** | Stage 0 | **Alpha (8–13 Hz)** & Beta (>13 Hz) | Eyes open → alpha blocking | — |
| **N1** | Stage 1 | **Theta (4–7 Hz)** | Slow eye movements, vertex sharp waves | **5 %** |
| **N2** | Stage 2 | Mixed theta + **sleep spindles (11–16 Hz)** + **K-complexes** | Memory consolidation marker | **45 %** |
| **N3** | Stages 3 & 4 | **Delta (0.5–4 Hz)** — Slow Wave Sleep (SWS) | ≥ 20 % delta waves per epoch | **20 %** |
| **R (REM)** | Stage REM | Low-voltage mixed-frequency (sawtooth waves) | Rapid eye movements, **EMG atonia** | **25 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REM is the only stage with explicit EMG atonia** (muscle paralysis). This is a **mechatronic-friendly feature** because a single chin-EMG channel can dramatically improve automated scoring accuracy.

## 2.2 Characteristic Waveform Glossary (Engineering-Relevant)

| Waveform | Frequency Band | Amplitude | Physiological Role | Sensor Localization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Beta (β)** | > 13 Hz | < 20 µV | Active thinking | Frontal EEG (Fp1, Fp2) |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8–13 Hz | 20–60 µV | Relaxed wakefulness | Occipital EEG (O1, O2) |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4–7 Hz | > 20 µV | Light sleep / drowsiness | Central EEG (C3, C4) |
| **Sleep Spindle** | 11–16 Hz | > 12 µV | Memory processing | Central EEG |
| **K-Complex** | 0.5–1.5 Hz biphasic | > 75 µV | Arousal suppression | Central EEG |
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5–4 Hz | > 75 µV | Deep SWS, restoration | Frontal EEG (F3, F4) |
| **Sawtooth** | 2–6 Hz | < 50 µV | REM marker | Central EEG |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are **directly applicable** for designing amplifiers, filters, and sleep-cycle calculators in biomechatronic projects.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{cycle} \approx 90 \text{ min}$ | Average adult sleep cycle | minutes |
| 2 | $N_{cycles} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Number of cycles per night | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_{delta} \in [0.5,\ 4]\ \text{Hz}$ | SWS dominant band | Hz |
| 4 | $f_{spindle} \in [11,\ 16]\ \text{Hz}$ | N2 marker band | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{sawtooth} \in [2,\ 6]\ \text{Hz}$ | REM sawtooth band | Hz |
| 6 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Criterion** for EEG sampling | Hz |
| 7 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Aliasing limit frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $V_{pp} = 2\sqrt{2}\, V_{rms}$ | Peak-to-peak from RMS (for EEG) | µV |
| 9 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | EEG amplifier SNR | dB |
| 10 | $\eta_{stage} = \dfrac{t_{stage}}{T_{total}} \times 100\ \%$ | Sleep efficiency per stage | % |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Band-pass filters must be tuned to the **delta, theta, spindle, and sawtooth** ranges to extract sleep micro-events.
- **Wearable Design:** Smart-watches use **actigraphy + HRV (heart rate variability)** as proxy signals — the design of HRV band-pass filters (LF: 0.04–0.15 Hz, HF: 0.15–0.4 Hz) is directly inspired by sleep-stage physiology.
- **Closed-Loop Devices:** Inspire Medical's hypoglossal nerve stimulator (for sleep apnea) uses **N2/N3 detection** to modulate stimulation strength — requiring real-time sleep staging inside an implantable pulse generator (IPG).
- **AI Models:** Modern CNNs (e.g., DeepSleepNet, U-Time) take raw EEG epochs as input and output stage probabilities — **KTU students can implement these on edge AI hardware**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Calculating Total Sleep Time Across Stages

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** An adult has the following sleep-stage durations during an 8-hour night:
> - $t_{N1} = 24\ \text{min}$
> - $t_{N2} = 216\ \text{min}$
> - $t_{N3} = 96\ \text{min}$
> - $t_{R} = 120\ \text{min}$
>
> **Find:** (a) Total Sleep Time (TST), (b) Sleep efficiency of each stage, (c) Number of complete cycles.

### Step 1 — Compute Total Sleep Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= t_{N1} + t_{N2} + t_{N3} + t_{R} \\
&= 24 + 216 + 96 + 120 \\
&= 456\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Total Sleep Time into Hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total}^{hr} &= \dfrac{T_{total}}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{456}{60} \\
&= 7.6\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Sleep-Stage Efficiency (η) for Each Stage

Using the formula $\eta_{stage} = \dfrac{t_{stage}}{T_{total}} \times 100\ \%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{N1} &= \dfrac{24}{456} \times 100\ \% \approx 5.26\ \% \\
\eta_{N2} &= \dfrac{216}{456} \times 100\ \% \approx 47.37\ \% \\
\eta_{N3} &= \dfrac{96}{456} \times 100\ \% \approx 21.05\ \% \\
\eta_{R}  &= \dfrac{120}{456} \times 100\ \% \approx 26.32\ \% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity Check:** $\eta_{N1} + \eta_{N2} + \eta_{N3} + \eta_{R} = 5.26 + 47.37 + 21.05 + 26.32 = 100.00\ \text{\%}$ ✓

### Step 4 — Compute Number of Complete Cycles

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cycles} &= \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_{cycle}} \\
&= \dfrac{456\ \text{min}}{90\ \text{min/cycle}} \\
&= 5.067\ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the night lasted 8 h = 480 min, only $5$ **complete** cycles fit.

## 3.2 Derivation — Nyquist Sampling Criterion for EEG Sleep Monitoring

> **Problem Statement:** A polysomnograph must capture delta waves at $f_{max} = 30\ \text{Hz}$ (worst-case EEG bandwidth). Determine the minimum sampling frequency and the corresponding Nyquist frequency.

### Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist Theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &\geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \\
f_s &\geq 2 \times 30\ \text{Hz} \\
f_s &\geq 60\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Nyquist Frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{Nyquist} &= \dfrac{f_s}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{60}{2} \\
&= 30\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Clinical Over-Sampling Factor

Most clinical PSGs oversample by a factor of **4× to 8×** to preserve phase information in K-complexes and sleep spindles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{s,clinical} &= 8 \times f_{max} \\
&= 8 \times 30 \\
&= 240\ \text{Hz} \quad (\text{practical choice}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Modern digital PSGs use $f_s = 256\ \text{Hz}$ or $500\ \text{Hz}$, comfortably exceeding the requirement.

## 3.3 Derivation — Sleep Spindle Spectral Power Estimation

A sleep spindle is a 11–16 Hz burst lasting $\geq 0.5\ \text{s}$. The total energy $E_{spindle}$ in one burst can be approximated using Parseval's theorem:

$$
E_{spindle} = \int_{11}^{16} \vert P(f) \vert^2 \, df
$$

For an idealized rectangular band of amplitude $A\ \mu V$ over the spindle band:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{spindle} &= A^2 \times (16 - 11) \\
&= A^2 \times 5\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This simplified spectral energy metric is exactly what **DeepSleepNet** and similar CNN models compute as input features — making this derivation directly relevant to **AI-based sleep scoring** that KTU students may implement in capstone projects.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stage-Wise Sensor & Filter Design

| Stage | Key Sensor | Recommended Band-Pass Filter | Notch Filter (50/60 Hz) | Gain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **W** | EEG (O1, O2) + EOG | 0.5 – 30 Hz | 50 Hz | 10,000 × |
| **N1** | EEG (C3, C4) | 0.5 – 20 Hz | 50 Hz | 10,000 × |
| **N2** | EEG + chin EMG | 0.5 – 30 Hz (spindle bandpass 11–16 Hz) | 50 Hz | 10,000 × |
| **N3** | EEG (F3, F4) | 0.1 – 4 Hz (delta extraction) | 50 Hz | 20,000 × |
| **R** | EEG + EOG + chin EMG | 0.5 – 30 Hz | 50 Hz | 10,000 × |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PSG-Based Sleep Stager

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **signal-flow architecture** of a mechatronic polysomnography system — directly mapping how multi-modal biosignals are acquired, conditioned, and classified into sleep stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Subject / Patient] --> B1[EEG Electrodes Fp1 Fp2 C3 C4 O1 O2]
    A1 --> B2[EOG Electrodes E1 E2]
    A1 --> B3[Chin EMG Electrodes]
    B1 --> C1[Instrumentation Amplifier AD8221 Gain 10000]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 30 Hz]
    D1 --> E1[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    E1 --> F1[16 bit ADC 256 Hz]
    F1 --> G1[Microcontroller STM32 DSP Core]
    G1 --> H1[FFT Feature Extractor]
    H1 --> H2[Time Domain Features]
    H2 --> I1[Sleep Stage Classifier CNN LSTM]
    I1 --> J1[Output W N1 N2 N3 R]
    J1 --> K1[LCD Display and SD Card Logger]
    J1 --> K2[Wireless BLE Wi Fi Transmitter]
    G1 --> L1[Safety Isolator Optocoupler IEC 60601]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Signals flow from the patient → electrodes → differential amplifier → filtering → ADC → embedded DSP → AI classifier → display/cloud. The **optocoupler block** enforces **patient isolation**, a mandatory safety requirement under **IEC 60601-1** for any medical mechatronic device.

## 4.2 Sleep Cycle Architecture (Hypnogram-Inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Bedtime Wake W] --> S1[Stage N1 Theta 5 percent]
    S1 --> S2[Stage N2 Spindles 45 percent]
    S2 --> S3[Stage N3 Delta 20 percent]
    S3 --> S4[Stage R REM 25 percent]
    S4 --> S5[Cycle 1 Complete 90 min]
    S5 --> S6[Cycle 2 N1 N2 R N3 reduced]
    S6 --> S7[Cycle 3 to 5 REM dominant later cycles]
    S7 --> S8[Final Awakening W]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** In the first half of the night, **N3 (deep sleep) dominates**; in the second half, **REM sleep dominates**. This **asymmetry** is critical for **closed-loop drug delivery devices** that release melatonin or growth hormone at specific stages.

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Automated Sleep Scoring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0[Epoch Start 30 sec EEG EOG EMG] --> Y1{Alpha rhythm present}
    Y1 -- Yes --> Y2[Classify as Wake W]
    Y1 -- No --> Y3{Theta rhythm and slow eye movements}
    Y3 -- Yes --> Y4[Classify as Stage N1]
    Y3 -- No --> Y5{Sleep spindles or K complex present}
    Y5 -- Yes --> Y6[Classify as Stage N2]
    Y5 -- No --> Y7{Delta waves greater than 20 percent of epoch}
    Y7 -- Yes --> Y8[Classify as Stage N3]
    Y7 -- No --> Y9{Low EMG tone and rapid eye movements}
    Y9 -- Yes --> Y10[Classify as Stage R REM]
    Y9 -- No --> Y11[Unscored Artifact Flag]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **simplified expert-system flowchart** that mirrors the AASM decision tree. Modern **CNN classifiers** effectively learn this hierarchy as feature detectors — the **first layers** of the CNN learn spindle/K-complex detectors, while **deeper layers** learn REM signatures.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Sleep Stage" and list the five stages recognized by the AASM scoring manual.**

**Model Answer:**

A *sleep stage* is a clinically defined epoch of sleep distinguished by characteristic EEG, EOG, and EMG patterns, conventionally scored over a 30-second window. According to the **AASM Manual (2017)**, the five recognized stages are:

1. **W** — Wakefulness (alpha rhythm dominant)
2. **N1** — NREM Stage 1 (theta rhythm, drowsiness)
3. **N2** — NREM Stage 2 (sleep spindles, K-complexes)
4. **N3** — NREM Stage 3 (delta waves, slow-wave sleep)
5. **R** — REM Sleep (sawtooth waves, REM, EMG atonia)

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for list with one-line features)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of "Sleep Spindles" and "K-Complexes" in sleep stage identification.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Sleep Spindles:** Burst of 11–16 Hz waves lasting $\geq 0.5$ s, generated by the thalamic reticular nucleus. They are the **signature marker of Stage N2** and play a critical role in **memory consolidation** and **sensory gating**.
- **K-Complexes:** Biphasic waves of $\geq 0.5$ s duration with a sharp negative deflection followed by a slower positive component (75 µV amplitude). They suppress cortical arousal in response to external stimuli, marking the **transition into stable Stage N2**.

Both are detectable on central EEG channels (C3, C4) using a band-pass filter in the 11–16 Hz range.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each)*

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice provided. Each question carries sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **Polysomnography (PSG) system** used for sleep staging. List the **biosignals acquired** and the **standard electrode placements**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **EEG characteristics of each sleep stage** (W, N1, N2, N3, R) using frequency bands and waveforms. Include a table of typical amplitude ranges. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

A PSG system is a multi-channel biopotential acquisition system with the following architecture:

1. **Biosignals Acquired:**
   - **EEG** — 6 channels (Fp1, Fp2, C3, C4, O1, O2) per AASM
   - **EOG** — 2 channels (left & right outer canthi)
   - **EMG** — 1 chin channel + optional limb leads
   - **Auxiliary** — ECG, nasal airflow, thoracic effort, SpO₂, body position

2. **Standard Electrode Placement:** Follows the **International 10–20 System**:
   - **C3, C4** — Central (sleep spindle detection)
   - **F3, F4** — Frontal (delta wave detection)
   - **O1, O2** — Occipital (alpha rhythm)
   - **E1, E2** — 1 cm above/below outer canthus
   - **Chin EMG** — submental muscle group

3. **Signal Conditioning Chain:**
   Electrodes → Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128, gain = 10,000) → Band-Pass Filter (0.5–30 Hz) → 50 Hz Notch → 16-bit ADC @ 256 Hz → Microcontroller/DSP → Display + Storage

4. **Safety & Isolation:** Patient isolation via **optocoupler / medical-grade isolation barrier** per **IEC 60601-1**.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

| Stage | Dominant Frequency Band | Key Waveform | Amplitude (µV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **W** | Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (>13 Hz) | Alpha rhythm | 20 – 60 |
| **N1** | Theta (4–7 Hz) | Vertex sharp waves, slow eye movements | 20 – 50 |
| **N2** | Theta + Spindles + K-complexes | Sleep spindles (11–16 Hz), K-complexes | 30 – 75 |
| **N3** | Delta (0.5–4 Hz) | High-amplitude slow waves | > 75 |
| **R** | Low-voltage mixed | Sawtooth waves (2–6 Hz), REM | < 50 |

> A single 30-second epoch is scored as belonging to one stage based on the **dominant rhythm** and presence of micro-events.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **physiological changes** that occur during **REM sleep**, with particular reference to autonomic, muscular, and ocular activity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient undergoes an overnight PSG study for **8 hours**. The hypnogram reports the following stage durations: N1 = 18 min, N2 = 240 min, N3 = 90 min, REM = 132 min. Calculate the **(i)** Total Sleep Time, **(ii)** percentage of TST in each stage, and **(iii)** number of complete sleep cycles. Comment on the **clinical normality** of the result. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**REM (Rapid Eye Movement) Sleep — Physiological Profile:**

| System | Activity | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Brain (EEG)** | Low-voltage, mixed-frequency, sawtooth waves (2–6 Hz) | Drives AI classifiers' sawtooth detector |
| **Eyes (EOG)** | Bursts of rapid conjugate movements | Detected by EOG electrodes at outer canthi |
| **Muscles (EMG)** | Profound **atonia** (paralysis) via pons-medullary inhibition of spinal α-motor neurons | Single chin-EMG channel can identify REM |
| **Autonomic** | Irregular HR, BP, RR; penile/clitoral tumescence | Cardiopulmonary coupling algorithms |
| **Endocrine** | High cortisol & testosterone surges | Hormonal biomarker integration |
| **Cognition** | Vivid dreaming, emotional memory consolidation | Target of memory-enhancement implants |

> **Clinical Note:** Loss of REM atonia causes **REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD)** — patients physically act out dreams. This is a **mechatronic diagnostic opportunity** via wearable accelerometers.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**(i) Total Sleep Time (TST):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= 18 + 240 + 90 + 132 \\
&= 480\ \text{minutes} \\
&= 8.0\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Sleep-Stage Percentages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{N1} &= \dfrac{18}{480} \times 100\ \% = 3.75\ \% \\
\eta_{N2} &= \dfrac{240}{480} \times 100\ \% = 50.00\ \% \\
\eta_{N3} &= \dfrac{90}{480} \times 100\ \% = 18.75\ \% \\
\eta_{R}  &= \dfrac{132}{480} \times 100\ \% = 27.50\ \% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Number of Complete Sleep Cycles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cycles} &= \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{480}{90} = 5.33\ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the patient completed **5 full cycles** in 8 hours.

**Clinical Comment:**
- N1 (3.75%) is **lower than typical** (5%) — patient transitioned to sleep rapidly (good sleep initiation).
- N2 (50%) is **slightly elevated** (typical ≈ 45%) — possibly compensated for slightly reduced N3.
- N3 (18.75%) is **within normal range** (15–25%).
- REM (27.5%) is **at the upper-normal range** (20–25%).
- **Overall:** Sleep architecture is **clinically normal** with intact slow-wave and REM distribution across 5 cycles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> **Common mistakes students make (resulting in 2–3 mark deductions):**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing R&K vs AASM nomenclature** — Old textbooks use "Stage 1, 2, 3, 4 + REM"; the KTU 2024 syllabus expects **N1, N2, N3, R** nomenclature.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting the 30-second epoch** rule — Every sleep stage classification is scored on a 30-sec window. Mentioning this is worth 1 extra mark.
> 3. ❌ **Not specifying electrode placement** — A block diagram without the **10–20 system** annotations loses marks.
> 4. ❌ **Skipping the Nyquist criterion** in design questions — Always state $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ for EEG amplifiers.
> 5. ❌ **Computing sleep efficiency incorrectly** — The formula is $\eta = \dfrac{t_{stage}}{T_{total}} \times 100\ \%$ (not against the time-in-bed).
> 6. ❌ **Missing the IEC 60601-1 isolation requirement** — Mechatronic devices touching the patient **must** have medical-grade isolation.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ Sleep is divided into **5 stages** (W, N1, N2, N3, R) as per AASM, scored on a **30-second epoch**.
- ✅ **N1** → Theta (4–7 Hz); **N2** → Spindles (11–16 Hz) + K-complexes; **N3** → Delta (0.5–4 Hz); **R** → Sawtooth + REM + EMG atonia.
- ✅ A typical adult cycle is **≈ 90 min**, repeated **4–6 times** per night.
- ✅ First half of the night = **N3 dominant**; second half = **REM dominant**.
- ✅ PSG acquires **EEG, EOG, chin EMG** as the minimum signal set.
- ✅ EEG amplifier design requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist); clinical PSGs oversample at **256–500 Hz**.
- ✅ EMG atonia in REM is the **single most reliable automated classifier feature**.
- ✅ Sleep staging is the foundation for **PSG devices, wearables, AI sleep classifiers, and closed-loop neuromodulators**.
- ✅ Mechatronic PSG hardware must comply with **IEC 60601-1** for patient safety/isolation.
- ✅ Sleep spindle and K-complex detection requires a **band-pass filter centered around 11–16 Hz**.
- ✅ Delta wave detection requires a **low-pass filter at ~4 Hz** on frontal EEG channels.
- ✅ REM percentage of TST typically lies in **20–25 %** — a deviation may indicate **narcolepsy, depression, or RBD**.
- ✅ Remember formulas: $N_{cycles} = T_{total}/T_{cycle}$, $\eta_{stage} = t_{stage}/T_{total}$, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 10-20 Electrode Placement System for EEG and EEG Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT861 - BIO MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** 10-20 Electrode Placement System for EEG and EEG Machine ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of EEG **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the electrical activity of the human brain. It measures the voltage fluctuations resulting from ionic current flows within the neurons of the brain, captured via multiple electrodes placed on the scalp surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** EEG is the biopotential recording of the brain's spontaneous electrical activity using surface scalp electrodes, characterized by a frequency range of **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz** and signal amplitude ranging from **1 µV to 200 µV**. The EEG was first recorded by the German psychiatrist **Hans Berger** in **1929**, who is regarded as the father of human EEG. Clinical EEG is used to diagnose conditions such as epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, brain tumors, and encephalitis. ### 1.2 Formal Definition of the 10-20 Electrode Placement System The **10-20 System** (also called the **International 10-20 System**) is the globally standardized method for positioning scalp electrodes during an EEG recording. It was formally adopted by the **International Federation of the Societies for Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology (IFSECN)** in **1958**, and remains the universal standard today (American Clinical Neurophysiology Society, 2006 revision). > [!NOTE] > The name "10-20" refers to the fact that electrodes are placed at distances of **10%** and **20%** of specific skull measurements from defined anatomical landmarks. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Microphones Around a Stadium" Imagine a football stadium where you want to localize where fans are cheering from. You would place microphones (sensors) in a regular, geometrically even pattern across the stadium so that each microphone captures a distinct, identifiable region. **The 10-20 system does exactly this with the human head:** - The **electrodes are like microphones** listening to tiny electrical "cheers" (neuronal firing) from different brain regions. - The **standardized placement** ensures that wherever the patient is, the same brain regions are recorded. - The **labels (Fp1, C3, O2, etc.)** are like the seat numbers of each microphone — instantly telling the doctor *where* on the head the signal originated. > [!TIP] > Without a standardized placement, comparing EEGs between two patients would be like comparing two football matches recorded by microphones placed at random positions — useless! ### 1.4 Key Anatomical Landmarks The 10-20 system uses **four reference points** on the skull: | Landmark | Anatomical Location | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Nasion** | Indentation between forehead and nose | Anterior midline reference | | **Inion** | Bony prominence at base of skull (occipital protuberance) | Posterior midline reference | | **Left Pre-auricular point (PAL)** | Small flap in front of left ear | Left lateral reference | | **Right Pre-auricular point (PAR)** | Small flap in front of right ear | Right lateral reference | ![Note] The **vertex (Cz)** is the central top point of the head, located at the intersection of two imaginary lines connecting these landmarks. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind the 10-20 Rule The human head varies considerably in size between adults, children, and across populations. A fixed-coordinate system (in centimeters) would be meaningless. Therefore, the 10-20 system uses **proportional (percentage-based)** placement — making it **scalp-size-independent**. **Core Principle:** Electrode positions are determined as percentages (10% or 20%) of the total measured distances between standard skull landmarks. ### 2.2 Two Key Measurement Axes **Axis 1 — Sagittal (Anteroposterior / Longitudinal):** Measured from **Nasion → Inion**, passing through the vertex. $$L_{longitudinal} = \text{Distance from Nasion to Inion (in cm)}$$ **Axis 2 — Coronal (Transverse / Lateral):** Measured from **Left Pre-auricular → Right Pre-auricular**, passing through the vertex. $$L_{transverse} = \text{Distance from Pre-auricular LEFT to Pre-auricular RIGHT (in cm)}$$ ### 2.3 Placement Logic — Step-by-Step **Along the Sagittal Axis (Nasion → Inion):** 1. **Fp (Frontopolar)** = at **10%** of $L_{longitudinal}$ from Nasion 2. **Fz (Frontal midline)** = at **20%** further (total 30% from Nasion) 3. **Cz (Central midline / Vertex)** = at **20%** further (total 50% from Nasion) 4. **Pz (Parietal midline)** = at **20%** further (total 70% from Nasion) 5. **Oz (Occipital midline)** = at **10%** back from Inion (total 90% from Nasion) **Along the Coronal Axis (Left → Right):** 1. **T3 (Temporal left)** = at **10%** of $L_{transverse}$ from PAL 2. **C3 (Central left)** = at **20%** further (total 30%) 3. **Cz (Vertex)** = at **20%** further (total 50%) 4. **C4 (Central right)** = at **20%** further (total 70%) 5. **T4 (Temporal right)** = at **10%** from PAR (total 90%) ### 2.4 Electrode Nomenclature — The "Address Code" Each electrode has a **three-part code** = **(Region Letter)(Number/Z)(Side Indicator)** **Region Letters:** - **Fp** — Frontopolar (prefrontal) - **F** — Frontal - **T** — Temporal - **C** — Central - **P** — Parietal - **O** — Occipital - **A** — Auricular (ear reference, A1 = left ear, A2 = right ear) **Numbering/Side Convention:** - **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9)** → **LEFT hemisphere** - **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8, 10)** → **RIGHT hemisphere** - **Letter "z"** (zero) → **MIDLINE** (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic:** "**L**eft = **O**dd" (L-O) and "**R**ight = **E**ven" (R-E). Easy way to remember in exam! ### 2.5 The Standard 21-Electrode Configuration A full clinical 10-20 EEG uses **21 electrodes** (including reference and ground): | Midline (z) | Left (odd) | Right (even) | |---|---|---| | Fpz | Fp1 | Fp2 | | Fz | F3 | F4 | | Cz | C3 | C4 | | Pz | P3 | P4 | | Oz | O1 | O2 | | — | T3 / T5 | T4 / T6 | | A1 (ear ref) | — | A2 (ear ref) | > [!NOTE] > The number of electrodes can be extended to **32, 64, 128, or 256** in high-density EEG research systems, but they all follow the proportional extension of the 10-20 base system. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Distance Fp → Oz (sagittal) | $L_{longitudinal}$ | cm | Total anteroposterior skull length | | Distance T3 → T4 (coronal) | $L_{transverse}$ | cm | Total lateral skull length | | Position of Fpz | $0.10 \cdot L_{longitudinal}$ from Nasion | cm | Frontopolar point | | Position of Cz (vertex) | $0.50 \cdot L_{longitudinal}$ from Nasion | cm | Topmost midline point | | Position of Oz | $0.10 \cdot L_{longitudinal}$ from Inion | cm | Occipital midline | | EEG Amplitude Range | $\mathbf{1 \; \mu V \; to \; 200 \; \mu V}$ | microvolts | Brain signal level | | EEG Frequency Range | $\mathbf{0.5 \; Hz \; to \; 100 \; Hz}$ | Hz | Spectral content | | Common Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | Noise rejection capability (typically **> 100 dB** for clinical EEG) | | Differential Gain Required | $A_d \approx 10{,}000 \; \text{to} \; 100{,}000$ | unitless | To amplify µV to mV range | | ADC Sampling Rate | $\mathbf{\geq 250 \; Hz}$ (clinical), $\mathbf{\geq 1000 \; Hz}$ (research) | samples/sec | Nyquist requirement | | Notch Filter Frequency | $\mathbf{50 \; Hz}$ (India/Europe) or $\mathbf{60 \; Hz}$ (USA) | Hz | Removes power-line interference | | Delta Wave Frequency | $\mathbf{0.5 - 4 \; Hz}$ | Hz | Deep sleep | | Theta Wave Frequency | $\mathbf{4 - 8 \; Hz}$ | Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep | | Alpha Wave Frequency | $\mathbf{8 - 13 \; Hz}$ | Hz | Relaxed wakefulness | | Beta Wave Frequency | $\mathbf{13 - 30 \; Hz}$ | Hz | Active thinking, alert | | Gamma Wave Frequency | $\mathbf{> 30 \; Hz}$ | Hz | Sensory processing, perception | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Clinical Diagnostics:** Epilepsy seizure localization, sleep disorder studies (polysomnography). - **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Devices like EEG-controlled wheelchairs, prosthetic arms, and P300 spellers for paralyzed patients. - **Neurofeedback Therapy:** Used in ADHD, anxiety, and peak-performance training. - **Cognitive Neuroscience Research:** Studying attention, memory, emotion. - **Lie Detection & Forensic Use:** Research-grade applications only. - **Bionic Engineering:** Foundation for closed-loop deep brain stimulators and responsive neurostimulation systems. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Electrode Positions for a Given Patient **Given:** - A patient has a measured Nasion → Inion distance of $L_{longitudinal} = 36 \text{ cm}$. - A measured Pre-auricular LEFT → Pre-auricular RIGHT distance of $L_{transverse} = 38 \text{ cm}$. - Goal: Determine the exact (x, y) coordinates of Fp1, F3, C3, P3, O1, T3, T5 along the **left hemisphere**. --- **Step 1: Identify the central reference (Vertex Cz)** The vertex lies at the **midpoint** of both the sagittal and coronal axes. $$y_{Cz} = 0.50 \cdot L_{longitudinal} = 0.50 \cdot 36 \text{ cm} = 18 \text{ cm from Nasion}$$ $$x_{Cz} = 0.50 \cdot L_{transverse} = 0.50 \cdot 38 \text{ cm} = 19 \text{ cm from PAL (LEFT)}$$ --- **Step 2: Calculate the position of Fpz (Frontopolar midline)** The Frontopolar point is located at **10%** of the sagittal distance from the Nasion. $$y_{Fpz} = 0.10 \cdot L_{longitudinal} = 0.10 \cdot 36 \text{ cm} = 3.6 \text{ cm from Nasion}$$ --- **Step 3: Calculate the position of Oz (Occipital midline)** The Occipital point is at **10%** of the sagittal distance **from the Inion**. $$y_{Oz} = L_{longitudinal} - 0.10 \cdot L_{longitudinal} = 0.90 \cdot 36 \text{ cm} = 32.4 \text{ cm from Nasion}$$ Equivalently: $32.4 \text{ cm from Nasion} = 3.6 \text{ cm anterior to Inion}$. ✓ --- **Step 4: Calculate the positions of Fp1, F3 (Left Hemisphere)** These lie along the sagittal axis on the left side at: - **Fp1** = at 10% of $L_{longitudinal}$ from Nasion, on the left coronal line. - **F3** = at 20% further down on the left side from Fp1. $$y_{Fp1} = 0.10 \cdot 36 = 3.6 \text{ cm (same y as Fpz)}$$ $$y_{F3} = (0.10 + 0.20) \cdot 36 = 0.30 \cdot 36 = 10.8 \text{ cm from Nasion}$$ --- **Step 5: Calculate C3 position (Left Central)** C3 lies on the **left coronal line** at the same y-coordinate as Cz (since it's a lateral version of Cz). $$y_{C3} = 0.50 \cdot 36 = 18 \text{ cm from Nasion}$$ $$x_{C3} = 0.10 \cdot L_{transverse} \cdot (\text{offset from PAL})$$ Actually, along the coronal axis: T3 = 10% from PAL, C3 = 20% further from T3. $$x_{T3} = 0.10 \cdot 38 = 3.8 \text{ cm from PAL}$$ $$x_{C3} = (0.10 + 0.20) \cdot 38 = 0.30 \cdot 38 = 11.4 \text{ cm from PAL}$$ --- **Step 6: Summary Table for this Patient** | Electrode | x (cm from PAL Left) | y (cm from Nasion) | Region | |---|---|---|---| | Fp1 | 19.0 (midline) | 3.6 | Frontopolar | | F3 | 11.4 | 10.8 | Frontal | | C3 | 11.4 | 18.0 | Central | | P3 | 11.4 | 25.2 | Parietal | | O1 | 19.0 (midline) | 32.4 | Occipital | | T3 | 3.8 | 18.0 | Temporal | | T5 | 3.8 | 25.2 | Posterior Temporal | --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum ADC Sampling Rate (Nyquist Theorem) **Problem:** EEG signals contain frequency components up to $f_{max} = 100 \text{ Hz}$. What is the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing? **Solution Steps:** According to the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**: $$f_{sample} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ Substituting $f_{max} = 100 \text{ Hz}$: $$f_{sample} \geq 2 \cdot 100 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_{sample} \geq 200 \text{ Hz}$$ In clinical EEG, **oversampling** is performed for accuracy: $$f_{sample, \; clinical} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_{sample, \; research} = 1000 \text{ Hz or higher}$$ > [!NOTE] > The actual selection in clinical practice is also governed by the **Sleep Medicine Society guidelines** and the requirement to capture fast transients (e.g., epileptic spikes). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Differential Amplifier Output **Problem:** Two electrodes measure scalp potentials $V_1$ and $V_2$. The differential amplifier with gain $A_d$ produces an output $V_{out}$. Derive the output expression. **Given:** - $V_1 = 50 \text{ µV}$ (actual brain signal) - $V_2 = 48 \text{ µV}$ (actual brain signal at second electrode) - Common mode noise $V_{cm} = 1000 \text{ µV}$ (50 Hz interference picked up equally by both electrodes) **Step 1:** Write the input to each terminal of the amplifier: $$V_{+} = V_1 + V_{cm} = 50 + 1000 = 1050 \; \mu V$$ $$V_{-} = V_2 + V_{cm} = 48 + 1000 = 1048 \; \mu V$$ **Step 2:** Compute the differential voltage: $$V_{diff} = V_{+} - V_{-} = 1050 - 1048 = 2 \; \mu V$$ **Step 3:** Compute the common-mode voltage: $$V_{common} = \frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2} = \frac{1050 + 1048}{2} = 1049 \; \mu V$$ **Step 4:** Apply the amplifier transfer equation: $$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{diff} + A_{cm} \cdot V_{common}$$ For an ideal differential amplifier with $A_d = 10{,}000$ and finite $A_{cm} = 1$ (CMRR = 80 dB): $$V_{out} = 10{,}000 \cdot 2 \; \mu V + 1 \cdot 1049 \; \mu V$$ $$V_{out} = 20{,}000 \; \mu V + 1{,}049 \; \mu V = 21{,}049 \; \mu V \approx 21.05 \; mV$$ > [!TIP] > If CMRR were infinite ($A_{cm} = 0$), output would be a clean $20 \text{ mV}$. The extra $1.05 \text{ mV}$ represents the residual common-mode noise — minimized by high-CMRR instrumentation amplifiers like the AD620 or INA128. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Bands ↔ Brain States ↔ Clinical Significance | Band | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (µV) | Brain State | Clinical Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 100 – 200 | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infancy | Excess in awake adults → brain damage, tumors | | **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | < 30 | Drowsiness, meditation, light sleep | Focal theta → focal lesion, deep midline lesions | | **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed, eyes closed, awake | Posterior dominant rhythm (PDR); absent alpha → cerebral dysfunction | | **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | < 20 | Active thinking, alert, anxious | Drug-induced beta (sedatives), frontal in normal | | **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | Very low | Perception, cognition, learning | Research; correlates with conscious awareness | | **Mu (µ)** | 8 – 12 | — | Motor cortex idling | Suppressed by movement/motor imagery (BCI applications) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete EEG Machine Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Scalp Surface
Brain Electrical Activity
1 to 200 microvolt] --> B[Electrodes
21 Ag or AgCl
10 to 20 placement system] B --> C[Electrode Paste
Conductive Gel
Reduces skin impedance] C --> D[Input Connector Box
Junction Box
Safety isolation] D --> E[Differential Amplifier
Gain 10000 to 100000
CMRR greater than 100 dB] E --> F[Band Pass Filter
0.5 Hz to 100 Hz
Removes DC and HF noise] F --> G[Notch Filter

Removes line interference] G --> H[Analog to Digital Converter
Sampling at 250 Hz minimum
16 bit or 24 bit resolution] H --> I[Microprocessor or DSP
Digital Signal Processing
